title: The Bureaucracy of Speed: Structural Equivalence Between Memory Consistency Models and Multi-Agent Authorization Revocation
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Simulation code at https://github.com/hipvlady/prizm
summary: The temporal assumptions underpinning conventional Identity and Access Management collapse under agentic execution regimes. A sixty-second revocation window permits on the order of $6 \times 10^3$ unauthorized API calls at 100 ops/tick; at AWS Lambda scale, the figure approaches $6 \times 10^5$. This is a coherence problem, not merely a latency problem. We define a Capability Coherence System (CCS) and construct a state-mapping $\varphi : Σ_{\rm MESI} \to Σ_{\rm auth}$ preserving transition structure under bounded-staleness semantics. A safety theorem bounds unauthorized operations for the execution-count Release Consistency-directed Coherence (RCC) strategy at $D_{\rm rcc} \leq n$, independent of agent velocity $v$ -- a qualitative departure from the $O(v \cdot \mathrm{TTL})$ scaling of time-bounded strategies. Tick-based discrete event simulation across three business-contextualised scenarios (four strategies, ten deterministic seeds each) confirms: RCC achieves a $120\times$ reduction versus TTL-based lease in the high-velocity scenario (50 vs. 6,000 unauthorized operations), and $184\times$ under anomaly-triggered revocation. Zero bound violations across all 120 runs confirm the per-capability safety guarantee. Simulation code: https://github.com/hipvlady/prizm

AI.tldr: A Capability Coherence System (CCS) using Release Consistency-directed Coherence (RCC) strategy eliminates time-based revocation vulnerabilities in agentic systems, bounding unauthorized operations independent of agent velocity.
AI.motivation: Traditional time-based access revocation fails in agentic execution regimes where milliseconds matter - a 60-second revocation window can allow up to 600,000 unauthorized API calls at AWS Lambda scale. This is a coherence problem, not just latency.
AI.method: Developed a Capability Coherence System (CCS) with state-mapping φ from MESI protocol states to authorization states, preserving transition structure under bounded-staleness semantics. Introduced RCC strategy based on release consistency from computer architecture. Validated through tick-based discrete event simulation across three business scenarios with four strategies and deterministic seeds.
AI.result: RCC achieves 120x reduction in unauthorized operations vs TTL-based leases in high-velocity scenarios (50 vs 6,000 ops) and 184x reduction under anomaly-triggered revocation. Zero bound violations across 120 runs confirms per-capability safety guarantee independent of agent velocity.
AI.conclusion: The CCS with RCC strategy provides qualitatively superior security by bounding unauthorized operations independent of agent velocity, solving the coherence problem inherent in time-based revocation systems for agentic execution environments.

title: Emotional Modulation in Swarm Decision Dynamics
comment: Accepted for presentation at the International Conference on Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICAART 2026)
summary:
Collective decision-making in biological and human groups often emerges from simple interaction rules that amplify minor differences into consensus. The bee equation, developed initially to describe nest-site selection in honeybee swarms, captures this dynamic through recruitment and inhibition processes. Here, we extend the bee equation into an agent-based model in which emotional valence (positive-negative) and arousal (low-high) act as modulators of interaction rates, effectively altering the recruitment and cross-inhibition parameters. Agents display simulated facial expressions mapped from their valence-arousal states, allowing the study of emotional contagion in consensus formation.
  Three scenarios are explored: (1) the joint effect of valence and arousal on consensus outcomes and speed, (2) the role of arousal in breaking ties when valence is matched, and (3) the "snowball effect" in which consensus accelerates after surpassing intermediate support thresholds. Results show that emotional modulation can bias decision outcomes and alter convergence times by shifting effective recruitment and inhibition rates. At the same time, intrinsic non-linear amplification can produce decisive wins even in fully symmetric emotional conditions.
  These findings link classical swarm decision theory with affective and social modelling, highlighting how both emotional asymmetries and structural tipping points shape collective outcomes. The proposed framework offers a flexible tool for studying the emotional dimensions of collective choice in both natural and artificial systems.

AI.tldr: Extended the bee equation into an agent-based model incorporating emotional valence and arousal as modulators of interaction rates, showing how emotional factors bias collective decisions and convergence times.
AI.motivation: To bridge classical swarm decision theory with affective and social modeling by incorporating emotional dimensions (valence-arousal) into collective decision-making processes, moving beyond simple interaction rules to include emotional modulation effects.
AI.method: Developed an agent-based model extending the bee equation where emotional valence (positive/negative) and arousal (low/high) modulate interaction rates, altering recruitment and cross-inhibition parameters. Agents display simulated facial expressions mapped from valence-arousal states to study emotional contagion in consensus formation.
AI.result: 1) Emotional modulation biases decision outcomes and alters convergence times by shifting effective recruitment/inhibition rates. 2) Arousal breaks ties when valence is matched. 3) "Snowball effect" accelerates consensus after surpassing intermediate thresholds. 4) Nonlinear amplification can produce decisive wins even in symmetric emotional conditions.
AI.conclusion: Emotional asymmetries and structural tipping points both shape collective outcomes, linking swarm decision theory with affective modeling. The framework provides a flexible tool for studying emotional dimensions of collective choice in natural and artificial systems.

title: LDP: An Identity-Aware Protocol for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: As multi-agent AI systems grow in complexity, the protocols connecting them constrain their capabilities. Current protocols such as A2A and MCP do not expose model-level properties as first-class primitives, ignoring properties fundamental to effective delegation: model identity, reasoning profile, quality calibration, and cost characteristics. We present the LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP), an AI-native communication protocol introducing five mechanisms: (1) rich delegate identity cards with quality hints and reasoning profiles; (2) progressive payload modes with negotiation and fallback; (3) governed sessions with persistent context; (4) structured provenance tracking confidence and verification status; (5) trust domains enforcing security boundaries at the protocol level. We implement LDP as a plugin for the JamJet agent runtime and evaluate against A2A and random baselines using local Ollama models and LLM-as-judge evaluation. Identity-aware routing achieves ~12x lower latency on easy tasks through delegate specialization, though it does not improve aggregate quality in our small delegate pool; semantic frame payloads reduce token count by 37% (p=0.031) with no observed quality loss; governed sessions eliminate 39% token overhead at 10 rounds; and noisy provenance degrades synthesis quality below the no-provenance baseline, arguing that confidence metadata is harmful without verification. Simulated analyses show architectural advantages in attack detection (96% vs. 6%) and failure recovery (100% vs. 35% completion). This paper contributes a protocol design, reference implementation, and initial evidence that AI-native protocol primitives enable more efficient and governable delegation.

AI.tldr: LDP introduces an AI-native protocol with identity-aware routing, semantic payloads, governed sessions, provenance tracking, and trust domains to improve multi-agent system efficiency and governance.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent AI protocols (A2A, MCP) fail to expose model-level properties as first-class primitives, ignoring essential delegation properties like model identity, reasoning profiles, quality calibration, and cost characteristics that are fundamental to effective agent-to-agent delegation.
AI.method: The LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP) introduces five key mechanisms: 1) rich delegate identity cards with quality hints and reasoning profiles, 2) progressive payload modes with negotiation and fallback, 3) governed sessions with persistent context, 4) structured provenance tracking confidence and verification status, and 5) trust domains enforcing security boundaries at the protocol level. Implemented as a JamJet plugin and evaluated against A2A and random baselines using local Ollama models and LLM-as-judge evaluation.
AI.result: Identity-aware routing achieved ~12x lower latency on easy tasks through delegate specialization; semantic frame payloads reduced token count by 37% with no quality loss (p=0.031); governed sessions eliminated 39% token overhead at 10 rounds; noisy provenance degraded synthesis quality below baseline, suggesting confidence metadata is harmful without verification. Simulation showed 96% attack detection (vs. 6%) and 100% failure recovery (vs. 35% completion).
AI.conclusion: AI-native protocol primitives enable more efficient and governable delegation in multi-agent systems, addressing fundamental limitations of current protocols through specialized identity-aware routing, semantic payload optimization, session governance, and security-enhancing trust domains.

title: Chaotic Dynamics in Multi-LLM Deliberation
comment: Main text: 6 pages, 4 figures; Supplementary Information: 14 pages, 7 supplementary figures
summary: Collective AI systems increasingly rely on multi-LLM deliberation, but their stability under repeated execution remains poorly characterized. We model five-agent LLM committees as random dynamical systems and quantify inter-run sensitivity using an empirical Lyapunov exponent ($\hatλ$) derived from trajectory divergence in committee mean preferences. Across 12 policy scenarios, a factorial design at $T=0$ identifies two independent routes to instability: role differentiation in homogeneous committees and model heterogeneity in no-role committees. Critically, these effects appear even in the $T=0$ regime where practitioners often expect deterministic behavior. In the HL-01 benchmark, both routes produce elevated divergence ($\hatλ=0.0541$ and $0.0947$, respectively), while homogeneous no-role committees also remain in a positive-divergence regime ($\hatλ=0.0221$). The combined mixed+roles condition is less unstable than mixed+no-role ($\hatλ=0.0519$ vs $0.0947$), showing non-additive interaction. Mechanistically, Chair-role ablation reduces $\hatλ$ most strongly, and targeted protocol variants that shorten memory windows further attenuate divergence. These results support stability auditing as a core design requirement for multi-LLM governance systems.

AI.tldr: Multi-LLM committees exhibit instability even at T=0 due to role differentiation and model heterogeneity, requiring stability auditing for governance systems.
AI.motivation: Collective AI systems increasingly use multi-LLM deliberation, but their stability under repeated execution remains poorly characterized. Practitioners often expect deterministic behavior at T=0 (temperature=0), but this assumption may not hold for complex multi-agent systems.
AI.method: The authors model five-agent LLM committees as random dynamical systems and quantify inter-run sensitivity using empirical Lyapunov exponent (λ̂) derived from trajectory divergence in committee mean preferences. They use a factorial design across 12 policy scenarios, examining four conditions: homogeneous no-role, homogeneous with roles, heterogeneous (mixed) no-role, and heterogeneous with roles.
AI.result: Two independent routes to instability were identified: (1) role differentiation in homogeneous committees and (2) model heterogeneity in no-role committees. Both produce elevated divergence (λ̂=0.0541 and 0.0947 respectively). Homogeneous no-role committees still show positive divergence (λ̂=0.0221). Combined mixed+roles is less unstable than mixed+no-role (λ̂=0.0519 vs 0.0947), showing non-additive interaction. Chair-role ablation reduces λ̂ most strongly, and shortening memory windows further attenuates divergence.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LLM committees demonstrate significant instability even at T=0, challenging assumptions about deterministic behavior. Stability auditing should be a core design requirement for multi-LLM governance systems, with protocol modifications (like memory window reduction) offering practical mitigation strategies.

title: MASEval: Extending Multi-Agent Evaluation from Models to Systems
summary:
The rapid adoption of LLM-based agentic systems has produced a rich ecosystem of frameworks (smolagents, LangGraph, AutoGen, CAMEL, LlamaIndex, i.a.). Yet existing benchmarks are model-centric: they fix the agentic setup and do not compare other system components. We argue that implementation decisions substantially impact performance, including choices such as topology, orchestration logic, and error handling. MASEval addresses this evaluation gap with a framework-agnostic library that treats the entire system as the unit of analysis. Through a systematic system-level comparison across 3 benchmarks, 3 models, and 3 frameworks, we find that framework choice matters as much as model choice. MASEval allows researchers to explore all components of agentic systems, opening new avenues for principled system design, and practitioners to identify the best implementation for their use case.
  MASEval is available under the MIT licence https://github.com/parameterlab/MASEval.

AI.tldr: MASEval is a framework-agnostic evaluation library for LLM-based agentic systems that treats the entire system as the unit of analysis, showing that framework choice matters as much as model choice.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based agentic systems are model-centric and fail to compare other system components, while implementation decisions (topology, orchestration logic, error handling) substantially impact performance.
AI.method: MASEval provides a framework-agnostic library that treats the entire agentic system as the unit of analysis, enabling systematic comparison across frameworks, models, and benchmarks.
AI.result: Through systematic comparison across 3 benchmarks, 3 models, and 3 frameworks, researchers found that framework choice matters as much as model choice in agentic system performance.
AI.conclusion: MASEval addresses the evaluation gap for agentic systems, enabling researchers to explore all system components for principled design and helping practitioners identify optimal implementations for their use cases.

title: Equitable Multi-Task Learning for AI-RANs
summary: AI-enabled Radio Access Networks (AI-RANs) are expected to serve heterogeneous users with time-varying learning tasks over shared edge resources. Ensuring equitable inference performance across these users requires adaptive and fair learning mechanisms. This paper introduces an online-within-online fair multi-task learning (OWO-FMTL) framework that ensures long-term equity across users. The method combines two learning loops: an outer loop updating the shared model across rounds and an inner loop rebalancing user priorities within each round with a lightweight primal-dual update. Equity is quantified via generalized alpha-fairness, allowing a trade-off between efficiency and fairness. The framework guarantees diminishing performance disparity over time and operates with low computational overhead suitable for edge deployment. Experiments on convex and deep learning tasks confirm that OWO-FMTL outperforms existing multi-task learning baselines under dynamic scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes OWO-FMTL, an online fair multi-task learning framework for AI-RANs that ensures long-term equity across heterogeneous users via two-layer learning loops and alpha-fairness optimization.
AI.motivation: AI-enabled Radio Access Networks need to serve diverse users with varying learning tasks over shared edge resources while ensuring equitable inference performance across all users, especially in dynamic environments.
AI.method: The method uses an online-within-online framework with two learning loops: an outer loop updates the shared model across rounds, while an inner loop rebalances user priorities within each round using lightweight primal-dual updates. Equity is quantified using generalized alpha-fairness.
AI.result: The framework guarantees diminishing performance disparity over time, operates with low computational overhead suitable for edge deployment, and outperforms existing multi-task learning baselines in both convex and deep learning tasks under dynamic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: OWO-FMTL provides an effective solution for fair resource allocation and learning in AI-RAN environments, balancing efficiency and fairness while maintaining computational feasibility for edge deployment.

title: Strategically Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning with Linear Function Approximation
summary: Provably efficient and robust equilibrium computation in general-sum Markov games remains a core challenge in multi-agent reinforcement learning. Nash equilibrium is computationally intractable in general and brittle due to equilibrium multiplicity and sensitivity to approximation error. We study Risk-Sensitive Quantal Response Equilibrium (RQRE), which yields a unique, smooth solution under bounded rationality and risk sensitivity. We propose \texttt{RQRE-OVI}, an optimistic value iteration algorithm for computing RQRE with linear function approximation in large or continuous state spaces. Through finite-sample regret analysis, we establish convergence and explicitly characterize how sample complexity scales with rationality and risk-sensitivity parameters. The regret bounds reveal a quantitative tradeoff: increasing rationality tightens regret, while risk sensitivity induces regularization that enhances stability and robustness. This exposes a Pareto frontier between expected performance and robustness, with Nash recovered in the limit of perfect rationality and risk neutrality. We further show that the RQRE policy map is Lipschitz continuous in estimated payoffs, unlike Nash, and RQRE admits a distributionally robust optimization interpretation. Empirically, we demonstrate that \texttt{RQRE-OVI} achieves competitive performance under self-play while producing substantially more robust behavior under cross-play compared to Nash-based approaches. These results suggest \texttt{RQRE-OVI} offers a principled, scalable, and tunable path for equilibrium learning with improved robustness and generalization.

AI.tldr: A new equilibrium concept (RQRE) and algorithm (RQRE-OVI) for multi-agent RL that provides unique, robust solutions with theoretical guarantees, addressing the brittleness of Nash equilibrium in general-sum Markov games.
AI.motivation: Nash equilibrium is computationally intractable, brittle (due to equilibrium multiplicity and sensitivity to approximation), and lacks robustness in general-sum Markov games, motivating the need for more practical and robust equilibrium concepts.
AI.method: Proposes Risk-Sensitive Quantal Response Equilibrium (RQRE) and RQRE-OVI algorithm using optimistic value iteration with linear function approximation for large/continuous state spaces, with finite-sample regret analysis and distributionally robust optimization interpretation.
AI.result: Established convergence with explicit sample complexity scaling with rationality/risk-sensitivity parameters, revealed tradeoff between rationality (tightens regret) and risk sensitivity (regularization for stability), showed Lipschitz continuity unlike Nash, and demonstrated competitive self-play performance with substantially better cross-play robustness.
AI.conclusion: RQRE-OVI offers a principled, scalable, tunable path for equilibrium learning with improved robustness and generalization, recovering Nash equilibrium in the limit of perfect rationality and risk neutrality while addressing its practical limitations.

title: Hindsight Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents often face significant credit assignment challenges in long-horizon, multi-step tasks due to sparse rewards. Existing value-free methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), encounter two fundamental bottlenecks: inaccurate step-level Q-value estimation and misaligned value baselines for intermediate states. To address these limitations, we introduce HCAPO, the first framework to integrate hindsight credit assignment into LLM agents. HCAPO leverages the LLM itself as a post-hoc critic to refine step-level Q-values through hindsight reasoning. Furthermore, HCAPO's multi-scale advantage mechanism effectively supplements the inaccurate value baselines at critical decision states. Evaluations across three challenging benchmarks, including WebShop and ALFWorld, demonstrate that HCAPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art RL methods. Notably, HCAPO achieves a 7.7% improvement in success rate on WebShop and a 13.8% on ALFWorld over GRPO using the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model. These results indicate that HCAPO significantly enhances exploration efficiency, promotes concise decision-making, and ensures scalability in complex, long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: HCAPO is a new framework that integrates hindsight credit assignment into LLM agents to solve credit assignment challenges in long-horizon tasks, outperforming existing methods by 7.7-13.8% on benchmarks like WebShop and ALFWorld.
AI.motivation: LLM agents face significant credit assignment challenges in long-horizon, multi-step tasks due to sparse rewards. Existing value-free methods like GRPO have two fundamental bottlenecks: inaccurate step-level Q-value estimation and misaligned value baselines for intermediate states.
AI.method: HCAPO integrates hindsight credit assignment into LLM agents by using the LLM itself as a post-hoc critic to refine step-level Q-values through hindsight reasoning. It also employs a multi-scale advantage mechanism to supplement inaccurate value baselines at critical decision states.
AI.result: HCAPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art RL methods across three challenging benchmarks (WebShop, ALFWorld, and another unspecified). It achieves a 7.7% improvement in success rate on WebShop and 13.8% on ALFWorld over GRPO using the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model.
AI.conclusion: HCAPO significantly enhances exploration efficiency, promotes concise decision-making, and ensures scalability in complex, long-horizon tasks by effectively addressing credit assignment challenges through hindsight reasoning and multi-scale advantage mechanisms.

title: Context Engineering: From Prompts to Corporate Multi-Agent Architecture
summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems evolve from stateless chatbots to autonomous multi-step agents, prompt engineering (PE), the discipline of crafting individual queries, proves necessary but insufficient. This paper introduces context engineering (CE) as a standalone discipline concerned with designing, structuring, and managing the entire informational environment in which an AI agent makes decisions. Drawing on vendor architectures (Google ADK, Anthropic, LangChain), current academic work (ACE framework, Google DeepMind's intelligent delegation), enterprise research (Deloitte, 2026; KPMG, 2026), and the author's experience building a multi-agent system, the paper proposes five context quality criteria: relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, and provenance, and frames context as the agent's operating system. Two higher-order disciplines follow. Intent engineering (IE) encodes organizational goals, values, and trade-off hierarchies into agent infrastructure. Specification engineering (SE) creates a machine-readable corpus of corporate policies and standards enabling autonomous operation of multi-agent systems at scale. Together these four disciplines form a cumulative pyramid maturity model of agent engineering, in which each level subsumes the previous one as a necessary foundation. Enterprise data reveals a gap: while 75% of enterprises plan agentic AI deployment within two years (Deloitte, 2026), deployment has surged and retreated as organizations confront scaling complexity (KPMG, 2026). The Klarna case illustrates a dual deficit, contextual and intentional. Whoever controls the agent's context controls its behavior; whoever controls its intent controls its strategy; whoever controls its specifications controls its scale.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a maturity model for agent engineering with four disciplines: prompt engineering, context engineering, intent engineering, and specification engineering, forming a pyramid where each level builds on the previous as foundational layers for scalable AI agent deployment.
AI.motivation: As AI evolves from stateless chatbots to autonomous multi-step agents, traditional prompt engineering proves insufficient. Enterprises face scaling challenges with agentic AI deployment - while 75% plan deployment within two years, actual deployments have surged and retreated due to complexity. The Klarna case illustrates deficits in both contextual and intentional engineering.
AI.method: The paper synthesizes insights from vendor architectures (Google ADK, Anthropic, LangChain), academic work (ACE framework, Google DeepMind's intelligent delegation), enterprise research (Deloitte 2026, KPMG 2026), and the author's experience building multi-agent systems. It proposes five context quality criteria: relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, and provenance.
AI.result: The paper develops a cumulative pyramid maturity model comprising four disciplines: prompt engineering (crafting queries), context engineering (managing informational environment), intent engineering (encoding organizational goals), and specification engineering (creating machine-readable policies). Context engineering is framed as the agent's operating system.
AI.conclusion: Whoever controls the agent's context controls its behavior; whoever controls its intent controls its strategy; whoever controls its specifications controls its scale. The four disciplines form a necessary foundation for successful large-scale deployment of autonomous AI agents in enterprise settings.

title: Quantifying the Accuracy and Cost Impact of Design Decisions in Budget-Constrained Agentic LLM Search
comment: Accepted in 2026 Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC)
summary: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems combine iterative search, planning prompts, and retrieval backends, but deployed settings impose explicit budgets on tool calls and completion tokens. We present a controlled measurement study of how search depth, retrieval strategy, and completion budget affect accuracy and cost under fixed constraints. Using Budget-Constrained Agentic Search (BCAS), a model-agnostic evaluation harness that surfaces remaining budget and gates tool use, we run comparisons across six LLMs and three question-answering benchmarks. Across models and datasets, accuracy improves with additional searches up to a small cap, hybrid lexical and dense retrieval with lightweight re-ranking produces the largest average gains in our ablation grid, and larger completion budgets are most helpful on HotpotQA-style synthesis. These results provide practical guidance for configuring budgeted agentic retrieval pipelines and are accompanied by reproducible prompts and evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a measurement study showing that agentic RAG systems improve accuracy with additional searches up to a point, hybrid retrieval works best, and larger completion budgets help most with synthesis tasks.
AI.motivation: Agentic RAG systems combine iterative search, planning prompts, and retrieval, but deployed settings have explicit budgets on tool calls and tokens. There's a need to understand how search depth, retrieval strategy, and completion budget affect accuracy and cost under fixed constraints.
AI.method: The authors use Budget-Constrained Agentic Search (BCAS), a model-agnostic evaluation harness that surfaces remaining budget and gates tool use. They run comparisons across six LLMs and three question-answering benchmarks to measure effects.
AI.result: Across models and datasets: 1) accuracy improves with additional searches up to a small cap, 2) hybrid lexical and dense retrieval with lightweight re-ranking produces the largest average gains, and 3) larger completion budgets are most helpful on HotpotQA-style synthesis tasks.
AI.conclusion: The results provide practical guidance for configuring budgeted agentic retrieval pipelines, with reproducible prompts and evaluation settings included for implementation.

title: Generalized Reduction to the Isotropy for Flexible Equivariant Neural Fields
summary: Many geometric learning problems require invariants on heterogeneous product spaces, i.e., products of distinct spaces carrying different group actions, where standard techniques do not directly apply. We show that, when a group $G$ acts transitively on a space $M$, any $G$-invariant function on a product space $X \times M$ can be reduced to an invariant of the isotropy subgroup $H$ of $M$ acting on $X$ alone. Our approach establishes an explicit orbit equivalence $(X \times M)/G \cong X/H$, yielding a principled reduction that preserves expressivity. We apply this characterization to Equivariant Neural Fields, extending them to arbitrary group actions and homogeneous conditioning spaces, and thereby removing the major structural constraints imposed by existing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a mathematical framework to reduce invariant functions on product spaces to simpler subgroup actions, enabling more flexible equivariant neural fields.
AI.motivation: Many geometric learning problems require invariant functions on heterogeneous product spaces (products of distinct spaces with different group actions), but standard techniques don't directly apply to these cases. Existing methods for Equivariant Neural Fields impose major structural constraints.
AI.method: The authors show that when a group G acts transitively on space M, any G-invariant function on X×M can be reduced to an invariant of the isotropy subgroup H of M acting on X alone. They establish an explicit orbit equivalence (X×M)/G ≅ X/H, providing a principled reduction that preserves expressivity.
AI.result: The characterization enables application to Equivariant Neural Fields, extending them to arbitrary group actions and homogeneous conditioning spaces, thereby removing the structural constraints of existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a general mathematical framework that simplifies invariant function learning on product spaces, making equivariant neural networks more flexible and applicable to a wider range of geometric learning problems.

title: Influencing LLM Multi-Agent Dialogue via Policy-Parameterized Prompts
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a new paradigm for multi-agent systems. However, existing research on the behaviour of LLM-based multi-agents relies on ad hoc prompts and lacks a principled policy perspective. Different from reinforcement learning, we investigate whether prompt-as-action can be parameterized so as to construct a lightweight policy which consists of a sequence of state-action pairs to influence conversational behaviours without training. Our framework regards prompts as actions executed by LLMs, and dynamically constructs prompts through five components based on the current state of the agent. To test the effectiveness of parameterized control, we evaluated the dialogue flow based on five indicators: responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, and stance shift. We conduct experiments using different LLM-driven agents in two discussion scenarios related to the general public and show that prompt parameterization can influence the dialogue dynamics. This result shows that policy-parameterised prompts offer a simple and effective mechanism to influence the dialogue process, which will help the research of multi-agent systems in the direction of social simulation.

AI.tldr: LLM-based multi-agent systems can be controlled through parameterized prompts as lightweight policies without training, influencing dialogue dynamics effectively.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent research relies on ad hoc prompts and lacks a principled policy perspective, creating a need for systematic prompt control mechanisms.
AI.method: Treat prompts as actions executed by LLMs, dynamically constructing prompts through five components based on agent state to create a lightweight policy without training.
AI.result: Prompt parameterization effectively influences dialogue dynamics across five indicators (responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, stance shift) in two public discussion scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Policy-parameterized prompts offer a simple, effective mechanism to influence dialogue processes, advancing multi-agent systems research toward social simulation applications.

title: Interpretable Markov-Based Spatiotemporal Risk Surfaces for Missing-Child Search Planning with Reinforcement Learning and LLM-Based Quality Assurance
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures. Accepted at ICEIS 2026 (International Conference on Enterprise Information Systems)
summary: The first 72 hours of a missing-child investigation are critical for successful recovery. However, law enforcement agencies often face fragmented, unstructured data and a lack of dynamic, geospatial predictive tools. Our system, Guardian, provides an end-to-end decision-support system for missing-child investigation and early search planning. It converts heterogeneous, unstructured case documents into a schema-aligned spatiotemporal representation, enriches cases with geocoding and transportation context, and provides probabilistic search products spanning 0-72 hours. In this paper, we present an overview of Guardian as well as a detailed description of a three-layer predictive component of the system. The first layer is a Markov chain, a sparse, interpretable model with transitions incorporating road accessibility costs, seclusion preferences, and corridor bias with separate day/night parameterizations. The Markov chain's output prediction distributions are then transformed into operationally useful search plans by the second layer's reinforcement learning. Finally, the third layer's LLM performs post hoc validation of layer 2 search plans prior to their release. Using a synthetic but realistic case study, we report quantitative outputs across 24/48/72-hour horizons and analyze sensitivity, failure modes, and tradeoffs. Results show that the proposed predictive system with the three-layer architecture produces interpretable priors for zone optimization and human review.

AI.tldr: Guardian is an end-to-end decision-support system for missing-child investigations that converts unstructured case data into spatiotemporal representations and uses a three-layer predictive architecture (Markov chain, reinforcement learning, and LLM validation) to generate probabilistic search plans for the critical first 72 hours.
AI.motivation: The first 72 hours are critical for missing-child recovery, but law enforcement agencies lack dynamic, geospatial predictive tools and struggle with fragmented, unstructured data that hinders effective search planning.
AI.method: Guardian uses a three-layer predictive system: 1) A Markov chain model with day/night parameterizations that incorporates road accessibility costs, seclusion preferences, and corridor bias; 2) Reinforcement learning to transform Markov predictions into operational search plans; 3) LLM for post hoc validation of search plans before release.
AI.result: The system produces interpretable priors for zone optimization and human review across 24/48/72-hour horizons. Using a realistic case study, the paper reports quantitative outputs and analyzes sensitivity, failure modes, and tradeoffs of the proposed architecture.
AI.conclusion: Guardian successfully addresses the critical need for dynamic predictive tools in missing-child investigations by providing a comprehensive system that converts unstructured data into actionable search plans through a three-layer architecture that balances interpretability and operational utility.

title: SPREAD: Subspace Representation Distillation for Lifelong Imitation Learning
comment: IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: A key challenge in lifelong imitation learning (LIL) is enabling agents to acquire new skills from expert demonstrations while retaining prior knowledge. This requires preserving the low-dimensional manifolds and geometric structures that underlie task representations across sequential learning. Existing distillation methods, which rely on L2-norm feature matching in raw feature space, are sensitive to noise and high-dimensional variability, often failing to preserve intrinsic task manifolds. To address this, we introduce SPREAD, a geometry-preserving framework that employs singular value decomposition (SVD) to align policy representations across tasks within low-rank subspaces. This alignment maintains the underlying geometry of multimodal features, facilitating stable transfer, robustness, and generalization. Additionally, we propose a confidence-guided distillation strategy that applies a Kullback-Leibler divergence loss restricted to the top-M most confident action samples, emphasizing reliable modes and improving optimization stability. Experiments on the LIBERO, lifelong imitation learning benchmark, show that SPREAD substantially improves knowledge transfer, mitigates catastrophic forgetting, and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: SPREAD uses SVD for geometry-preserving distillation and confidence-guided KL divergence to improve lifelong imitation learning by maintaining task manifolds across sequential learning.
AI.motivation: Existing distillation methods in lifelong imitation learning fail to preserve low-dimensional manifolds and geometric structures of task representations when acquiring new skills, being sensitive to noise and high-dimensional variability.
AI.method: SPREAD employs singular value decomposition (SVD) to align policy representations across tasks within low-rank subspaces, preserving intrinsic geometry. Also uses confidence-guided distillation with KL divergence restricted to top-M most confident action samples.
AI.result: Experiments on LIBERO benchmark show SPREAD substantially improves knowledge transfer, mitigates catastrophic forgetting, and achieves state-of-the-art performance in lifelong imitation learning.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-preserving SPREAD framework successfully addresses key challenges in lifelong imitation learning by maintaining task manifolds and using confidence-guided distillation, enabling stable transfer and robustness.

title: AgentOS: From Application Silos to a Natural Language-Driven Data Ecosystem
summary: The rapid emergence of open-source, locally hosted intelligent agents marks a critical inflection point in human-computer interaction. Systems such as OpenClaw demonstrate that Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents can autonomously operate local computing environments, orchestrate workflows, and integrate external tools. However, within the current paradigm, these agents remain conventional applications running on legacy operating systems originally designed for Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) or Command Line Interfaces (CLIs). This architectural mismatch leads to fragmented interaction models, poorly structured permission management (often described as "Shadow AI"), and severe context fragmentation. This paper proposes a new paradigm: a Personal Agent Operating System (AgentOS). In AgentOS, traditional GUI desktops are replaced by a Natural User Interface (NUI) centered on a unified natural language or voice portal. The system core becomes an Agent Kernel that interprets user intent, decomposes tasks, and coordinates multiple agents, while traditional applications evolve into modular Skills-as-Modules enabling users to compose software through natural language rules. We argue that realizing AgentOS fundamentally becomes a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD) problem. The Agent Kernel must operate as a real-time engine for intent mining and knowledge discovery. Viewed through this lens, the operating system becomes a continuous data mining pipeline involving sequential pattern mining for workflow automation, recommender systems for skill retrieval, and dynamically evolving personal knowledge graphs. These challenges define a new research agenda for the KDD community in building the next generation of intelligent computing systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new paradigm called Personal Agent Operating System (AgentOS) to replace traditional GUI/CLI systems with a Natural User Interface and an Agent Kernel that treats operating system development as a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD) problem, creating a new research agenda for intelligent computing systems.
AI.motivation: The rapid emergence of open-source, locally hosted intelligent agents marks a critical inflection point in human-computer interaction. However, within the current paradigm, these agents remain conventional applications running on legacy operating systems originally designed for Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) or Command Line Interfaces (CLIs). This architectural mismatch leads to fragmented interaction models, poorly structured permission management (often described as "Shadow AI"), and severe context fragmentation.
AI.method: We argue that realizing AgentOS fundamentally becomes a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD) problem. The Agent Kernel must operate as a real-time engine for intent mining and knowledge discovery. Viewed through this lens, the operating system becomes a continuous data mining pipeline involving sequential pattern mining for workflow automation, recommender systems for skill retrieval, and dynamically evolving personal knowledge graphs.
AI.result: The paper proposes a new paradigm: a Personal Agent Operating System (AgentOS). In AgentOS, traditional GUI desktops are replaced by a Natural User Interface (NUI) centered on a unified natural language or voice portal. The system core becomes an Agent Kernel that interprets user intent, decomposes tasks, and coordinates multiple agents, while traditional applications evolve into modular Skills-as-Modules enabling users to compose software through natural language rules.
AI.conclusion: These challenges define a new research agenda for the KDD community in building the next generation of intelligent computing systems.

title: Multi-level meta-reinforcement learning with skill-based curriculum
summary: We consider problems in sequential decision making with natural multi-level structure, where sub-tasks are assembled together to accomplish complex goals. Systematically inferring and leveraging hierarchical structure has remained a longstanding challenge; we describe an efficient multi-level procedure for repeatedly compressing Markov decision processes (MDPs), wherein a parametric family of policies at one level is treated as single actions in the compressed MDPs at higher levels, while preserving the semantic meanings and structure of the original MDP, and mimicking the natural logic to address a complex MDP. Higher-level MDPs are themselves independent MDPs with less stochasticity, and may be solved using existing algorithms. As a byproduct, spatial or temporal scales may be coarsened at higher levels, making it more efficient to find long-term optimal policies. The multi-level representation delivered by this procedure decouples sub-tasks from each other and usually greatly reduces unnecessary stochasticity and the policy search space, leading to fewer iterations and computations when solving the MDPs. A second fundamental aspect of this work is that these multi-level decompositions plus the factorization of policies into embeddings (problem-specific) and skills (including higher-order functions) yield new transfer opportunities of skills across different problems and different levels. This whole process is framed within curriculum learning, wherein a teacher organizes the student agent's learning process in a way that gradually increases the difficulty of tasks and and promotes transfer across MDPs and levels within and across curricula. The consistency of this framework and its benefits can be guaranteed under mild assumptions. We demonstrate abstraction, transferability, and curriculum learning in examples, including MazeBase+, a more complex variant of the MazeBase example.

AI.tldr: A multi-level MDP compression method treats policies as actions in higher-level MDPs, reducing stochasticity and enabling efficient policy search. It supports skill transfer via policy factorization and is integrated with curriculum learning for gradual task difficulty increase.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of leveraging hierarchical structure in sequential decision making to efficiently solve complex Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Traditional methods often struggle with inferring and utilizing such structure systematically, leading to inefficiencies in policy search and handling of stochasticity. The motivation is to develop a multi-level compression procedure that preserves semantic meaning, reduces stochasticity, and enables transfer learning across tasks and levels.
AI.method: The method involves a multi-level compression of MDPs: policies at a given level are treated as single actions in a compressed MDP at a higher level, preserving semantic structure. Higher-level MDPs are independent with reduced stochasticity and can be solved using existing algorithms. Policies are factorized into problem-specific embeddings and reusable skills (including higher-order functions). This is integrated into a curriculum learning framework where a teacher agent organizes tasks to gradually increase difficulty and facilitate transfer across MDPs and levels.
AI.result: The authors propose a multi-level procedure for compressing MDPs, where policies at one level become actions in higher-level MDPs. This approach reduces stochasticity, coarsens spatial/temporal scales, and decouples sub-tasks, leading to fewer iterations and computations. The method also enables skill transfer across problems and levels via policy factorization into embeddings and skills. Framed within curriculum learning, the framework organizes learning to gradually increase task difficulty and promote transfer. Benefits include abstraction, transferability, and curriculum learning, demonstrated in examples like MazeBase+.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-level compression framework for MDPs effectively leverages hierarchical structure to improve efficiency in solving complex sequential decision-making problems. It reduces stochasticity, decouples sub-tasks, and shrinks the policy search space, leading to faster convergence. The factorization of policies enables transfer of skills across different problems and levels. When combined with curriculum learning, the approach promotes gradual learning and cross-task transfer, with guaranteed consistency under mild assumptions. Demonstrations in environments like MazeBase+ validate the benefits in abstraction, transferability, and curriculum learning.

title: A Consensus-Driven Multi-LLM Pipeline for Missing-Person Investigations
comment: Accepted to CAC: Applied Computing & Automation Conferences 2026. 16 pages, 6 figures
summary: The first 72 hours of a missing-person investigation are critical for successful recovery. Guardian is an end-to-end system designed to support missing-child investigation and early search planning. This paper presents the Guardian LLM Pipeline, a multi-model system in which LLMs are used for intelligent information extraction and processing related to missing-person search operations. The pipeline coordinates end-to-end execution across task-specialized LLM models and invokes a consensus LLM engine that compares multiple model outputs and resolves disagreements. The pipeline is further strengthened by QLoRA-based fine-tuning, using curated datasets. The presented design aligns with prior work on weak supervision and LLM-assisted annotation, emphasizing conservative, auditable use of LLMs as structured extractors and labelers rather than unconstrained end-to-end decision makers.

AI.tldr: Guardian is an end-to-end system using multi-model LLMs with consensus mechanisms and QLoRA fine-tuning to support missing-child investigations during critical first 72 hours.
AI.motivation: The first 72 hours of missing-person investigations are critical for successful recovery, especially for missing children. There's a need for intelligent systems to support investigation and early search planning.
AI.method: A multi-model LLM pipeline with task-specialized models, consensus LLM engine to resolve disagreements, and QLoRA-based fine-tuning using curated datasets. Uses LLMs as structured extractors and labelers rather than unconstrained decision makers.
AI.result: Presents the Guardian LLM Pipeline system designed to support missing-child investigation operations through intelligent information extraction and processing, with emphasis on conservative, auditable use of LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The system aligns with weak supervision and LLM-assisted annotation principles, positioning LLMs as structured tools for missing-person search operations rather than autonomous decision-makers.

title: The Temporal Markov Transition Field
summary: The Markov Transition Field (MTF), introduced by Wang and Oates (2015), encodes a time series as a two-dimensional image by mapping each pair of time steps to the transition probability between their quantile states, estimated from a single global transition matrix. This construction is efficient when the transition dynamics are stationary, but produces a misleading representation when the process changes regime over time: the global matrix averages across regimes and the resulting image loses all information about \emph{when} each dynamical regime was active. In this paper we introduce the \emph{Temporal Markov Transition Field} (TMTF), an extension that partitions the series into $K$ contiguous temporal chunks, estimates a separate local transition matrix for each chunk, and assembles the image so that each row reflects the dynamics local to its chunk rather than the global average. The resulting $T \times T$ image has $K$ horizontal bands of distinct texture, each encoding the transition dynamics of one temporal segment. We develop the formal definition, establish the key structural properties of the representation, work through a complete numerical example that makes the distinction from the global MTF concrete, analyse the bias--variance trade-off introduced by temporal chunking, and discuss the geometric interpretation of the local transition matrices in terms of process properties such as persistence, mean reversion, and trending behaviour. The TMTF is amplitude-agnostic and order-preserving, making it suitable as an input channel for convolutional neural networks applied to time series characterisation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Temporal Markov Transition Field (TMTF) as an improved version of MTF that preserves temporal information about when different dynamical regimes occur in time series by using local transition matrices instead of a single global average.
AI.motivation: The original Markov Transition Field (MTF) has a limitation: it uses a single global transition matrix that averages across different dynamical regimes, which loses information about when each regime was active in non-stationary time series where processes change over time.
AI.method: The TMTF partitions the time series into K contiguous temporal chunks, estimates a separate local transition matrix for each chunk, and assembles a T×T image where each row reflects the local dynamics of its chunk rather than the global average. This creates K horizontal bands with distinct textures encoding different temporal segments.
AI.result: The TMTF produces images with K horizontal bands of distinct texture, each encoding transition dynamics of one temporal segment. The method preserves temporal information about dynamical regimes, provides geometric interpretation of process properties (persistence, mean reversion, trending), and maintains amplitude-agnostic and order-preserving characteristics suitable for CNN input.
AI.conclusion: The Temporal Markov Transition Field (TMTF) successfully addresses the limitation of MTF by preserving temporal information about dynamical regimes in non-stationary time series, making it a more suitable representation for time series characterization tasks with convolutional neural networks.

title: The FABRIC Strategy for Verifying Neural Feedback Systems
summary: Forward reachability analysis is a dominant approach for verifying reach-avoid specifications in neural feedback systems, i.e., dynamical systems controlled by neural networks, and a number of directions have been proposed and studied. In contrast, far less attention has been given to backward reachability analysis for these systems, in part because of the limited scalability of known techniques. In this work, we begin to address this gap by introducing new algorithms for computing both over- and underapproximations of backward reachable sets for nonlinear neural feedback systems. We also describe and implement an integration of these backward reachability techniques with existing ones for forward analysis. We call the resulting algorithm Forward and Backward Reachability Integration for Certification (FaBRIC). We evaluate our algorithms on a representative set of benchmarks and show that they significantly outperform the prior state of the art.

AI.tldr: FaBRIC algorithm integrates forward and backward reachability analysis to improve certification of neural feedback systems, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Backward reachability analysis for neural feedback systems has received far less attention than forward analysis due to limited scalability of existing techniques, creating a significant gap in verification capabilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce new algorithms for computing both over- and underapproximations of backward reachable sets for nonlinear neural feedback systems, and integrate these with existing forward analysis techniques into a unified framework called FaBRIC.
AI.result: The algorithms significantly outperform the prior state of the art when evaluated on a representative set of benchmarks, demonstrating improved verification capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The integration of forward and backward reachability analysis in the FaBRIC framework addresses an important gap in neural feedback system verification and provides superior performance compared to existing methods.

title: SoftJAX & SoftTorch: Empowering Automatic Differentiation Libraries with Informative Gradients
summary: Automatic differentiation (AD) frameworks such as JAX and PyTorch have enabled gradient-based optimization for a wide range of scientific fields. Yet, many "hard" primitives in these libraries such as thresholding, Boolean logic, discrete indexing, and sorting operations yield zero or undefined gradients that are not useful for optimization. While numerous "soft" relaxations have been proposed that provide informative gradients, the respective implementations are fragmented across projects, making them difficult to combine and compare. This work introduces SoftJAX and SoftTorch, open-source, feature-complete libraries for soft differentiable programming. These libraries provide a variety of soft functions as drop-in replacements for their hard JAX and PyTorch counterparts. This includes (i) elementwise operators such as clip or abs, (ii) utility methods for manipulating Booleans and indices via fuzzy logic, (iii) axiswise operators such as sort or rank -- based on optimal transport or permutahedron projections, and (iv) offer full support for straight-through gradient estimation. Overall, SoftJAX and SoftTorch make the toolbox of soft relaxations easily accessible to differentiable programming, as demonstrated through benchmarking and a practical case study. Code is available at github.com/a-paulus/softjax and github.com/a-paulus/softtorch.

AI.tldr: SoftJAX and SoftTorch are open-source libraries providing soft differentiable alternatives to hard primitives in AD frameworks, enabling gradient-based optimization where standard libraries fail.
AI.motivation: Many "hard" primitives in AD frameworks like JAX and PyTorch (thresholding, Boolean logic, indexing, sorting) yield zero or undefined gradients, making them unsuitable for gradient-based optimization. Existing soft relaxations are fragmented across projects, making them difficult to combine and compare.
AI.method: Created SoftJAX and SoftTorch libraries that provide soft functions as drop-in replacements for hard JAX and PyTorch counterparts, including: (i) elementwise operators like clip/abs, (ii) Boolean/index manipulation via fuzzy logic, (iii) axiswise operators like sort/rank using optimal transport or permutahedron projections, and (iv) full support for straight-through gradient estimation.
AI.result: Developed feature-complete, open-source libraries that make soft relaxations easily accessible for differentiable programming, demonstrated through benchmarking and a practical case study. Code is available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: SoftJAX and SoftTorch provide a comprehensive toolbox of soft differentiable programming primitives that address limitations of standard AD frameworks, enabling gradient-based optimization for previously "hard" operations across scientific applications.

title: Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs) have shown strong performance in medical image understanding and clinical reasoning. Recent medical agent systems extend them with tool use and multi-agent collaboration, enabling complex decision-making. However, these systems rely almost entirely on frontier models (e.g., GPT), whose API-based deployment incurs high cost, high latency, and privacy risks that conflict with on-premise clinical requirements. We present Meissa, a lightweight 4B-parameter medical MM-LLM that brings agentic capability offline. Instead of imitating static answers, Meissa learns both when to engage external interaction (strategy selection) and how to execute multi-step interaction (strategy execution) by distilling structured trajectories from frontier models. Specifically, we propose: (1) Unified trajectory modeling: trajectories (reasoning and action traces) are represented within a single state-action-observation formalism, allowing one model to generalize across heterogeneous medical environments. (2) Three-tier stratified supervision: the model's own errors trigger progressive escalation from direct reasoning to tool-augmented and multi-agent interaction, explicitly learning difficulty-aware strategy selection. (3) Prospective-retrospective supervision: pairing exploratory forward traces with hindsight-rationalized execution traces enables stable learning of effective interaction policies. Trained on 40K curated trajectories, Meissa matches or exceeds proprietary frontier agents in 10 of 16 evaluation settings across 13 medical benchmarks spanning radiology, pathology, and clinical reasoning. Using over 25x fewer parameters than typical frontier models like Gemini-3, Meissa operates fully offline with 22x lower end-to-end latency compared to API-based deployment. Data, models, and environments are released at https://github.com/Schuture/Meissa.

AI.tldr: Meissa is a lightweight 4B-parameter medical multimodal LLM that enables offline agentic capabilities by distilling structured trajectories from frontier models, achieving comparable performance to proprietary models while reducing parameters by 25x and latency by 22x.
AI.motivation: Current medical agent systems rely heavily on frontier models like GPT through APIs, which creates three major problems: high cost, high latency, and privacy risks that conflict with clinical on-premise requirements. There's a need for lightweight, offline-capable models that can perform complex medical decision-making.
AI.method: Three key innovations: (1) Unified trajectory modeling - representing reasoning and action traces in a single state-action-observation formalism for cross-environment generalization; (2) Three-tier stratified supervision - progressive escalation from direct reasoning to tool-augmented and multi-agent interaction based on error detection; (3) Prospective-retrospective supervision - pairing exploratory forward traces with hindsight-rationalized execution traces for stable policy learning.
AI.result: Trained on 40K curated trajectories, Meissa matches or exceeds proprietary frontier agents in 10 of 16 evaluation settings across 13 medical benchmarks spanning radiology, pathology, and clinical reasoning. It operates with over 25x fewer parameters than typical frontier models and achieves 22x lower end-to-end latency compared to API-based deployment.
AI.conclusion: Meissa successfully demonstrates that lightweight, offline medical agent systems can achieve competitive performance with proprietary frontier models while addressing critical clinical requirements for cost, latency, and privacy protection.

title: Are Expressive Encoders Necessary for Discrete Graph Generation?
summary: Discrete graph generation has emerged as a powerful paradigm for modeling graph data, often relying on highly expressive neural backbones such as transformers or higher-order architectures. We revisit this design choice by introducing GenGNN, a modular message-passing framework for graph generation. Diffusion models with GenGNN achieve more than 90% validity on Tree and Planar datasets, within margins of graph transformers, at 2-5x faster inference speed. For molecule generation, DiGress with a GenGNN backbone achieves 99.49% Validity. A systematic ablation study shows the benefit provided by each GenGNN component, indicating the need for residual connections to mitigate oversmoothing on complicated graph-structure. Through scaling analyses, we apply a principled metric-space view to investigate learned diffusion representations and uncover whether GNNs can be expressive neural backbones for discrete diffusion.

AI.tldr: GenGNN is a modular message-passing framework for graph generation that achieves competitive results to transformers while being 2-5x faster, with ablation studies showing the importance of residual connections and investigating GNNs as expressive backbones for discrete diffusion.
AI.motivation: The paper revisits the common design choice of using highly expressive neural backbones like transformers for discrete graph generation, questioning whether simpler graph neural networks (GNNs) can serve as effective alternatives.
AI.method: Introduces GenGNN, a modular message-passing framework for graph generation. The method incorporates residual connections to mitigate oversmoothing and is used as a backbone for diffusion models. The paper includes systematic ablation studies and scaling analyses with a principled metric-space view to investigate learned diffusion representations.
AI.result: GenGNN achieves more than 90% validity on Tree and Planar datasets, within margins of graph transformers, while being 2-5x faster at inference. For molecule generation, DiGress with GenGNN achieves 99.49% Validity. Ablation studies show residual connections are crucial for mitigating oversmoothing on complex graph structures.
AI.conclusion: GNNs can serve as expressive neural backbones for discrete diffusion models in graph generation, offering competitive performance to transformers while being significantly faster. The modular GenGNN framework demonstrates this effectiveness through empirical results and systematic analysis of its components.

title: MEMO: Memory-Augmented Model Context Optimization for Robust Multi-Turn Multi-Agent LLM Games
summary: Multi-turn, multi-agent LLM game evaluations often exhibit substantial run-to-run variance. In long-horizon interactions, small early deviations compound across turns and are amplified by multi-agent coupling. This biases win rate estimates and makes rankings unreliable across repeated tournaments. Prompt choice worsens this further by producing different effective policies. We address both instability and underperformance with MEMO (Memory-augmented MOdel context optimization), a self-play framework that optimizes inference-time context by coupling retention and exploration. Retention maintains a persistent memory bank that stores structured insights from self-play trajectories and injects them as priors during later play. Exploration runs tournament-style prompt evolution with uncertainty-aware selection via TrueSkill, and uses prioritized replay to revisit rare and decisive states. Across five text-based games, MEMO raises mean win rate from 25.1% to 49.5% for GPT-4o-mini and from 20.9% to 44.3% for Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, using $2,000$ self-play games per task. Run-to-run variance also drops, giving more stable rankings across prompt variations. These results suggest that multi-agent LLM game performance and robustness have substantial room for improvement through context optimization. MEMO achieves the largest gains in negotiation and imperfect-information games, while RL remains more effective in perfect-information settings.

AI.tldr: MEMO (Memory-augmented MOdel context optimization) is a self-play framework that reduces run-to-run variance in multi-agent LLM game evaluations by optimizing inference-time context through memory retention and uncertainty-aware exploration.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn, multi-agent LLM game evaluations suffer from substantial run-to-run variance due to compounding small early deviations amplified by multi-agent coupling. This biases win rate estimates and makes rankings unreliable across repeated tournaments, with prompt choice exacerbating the problem by producing different effective policies.
AI.method: MEMO uses two key components: (1) Retention - maintains a persistent memory bank storing structured insights from self-play trajectories and injects them as priors during later play, and (2) Exploration - runs tournament-style prompt evolution with uncertainty-aware selection via TrueSkill, using prioritized replay to revisit rare and decisive states.
AI.result: Across five text-based games, MEMO raised mean win rate from 25.1% to 49.5% for GPT-4o-mini and from 20.9% to 44.3% for Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct using 2,000 self-play games per task. Run-to-run variance also dropped, giving more stable rankings across prompt variations. Largest gains were in negotiation and imperfect-information games.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent LLM game performance and robustness have substantial room for improvement through context optimization. MEMO demonstrates effectiveness in negotiation and imperfect-information settings, though RL remains more effective in perfect-information environments.

title: Expressivity-Efficiency Tradeoffs for Hybrid Sequence Models
summary: Hybrid sequence models--combining Transformer and state-space model layers--seek to gain the expressive versatility of attention as well as the computational efficiency of state-space model layers. Despite burgeoning interest in hybrid models, we lack a basic understanding of the settings where--and underlying mechanisms through which--they offer benefits over their constituent models. In this paper, we study this question, focusing on a broad family of core synthetic tasks. For this family of tasks, we prove the existence of fundamental limitations for non-hybrid models. Specifically, any Transformer or state-space model that solves the underlying task requires either a large number of parameters or a large working memory. On the other hand, for two prototypical tasks within this family--namely selective copying and associative recall--we construct hybrid models of small size and working memory that provably solve these tasks, thus achieving the best of both worlds. Our experimental evaluation empirically validates our theoretical findings. Importantly, going beyond the settings in our theoretical analysis, we empirically show that learned--rather than constructed--hybrids outperform non-hybrid models with up to 6x as many parameters. We additionally demonstrate that hybrid models exhibit stronger length generalization and out-of-distribution robustness than non-hybrids.

AI.tldr: Hybrid models combining Transformers and state-space models overcome fundamental limitations of non-hybrid models, achieving parameter efficiency, stronger length generalization, and better out-of-distribution robustness.
AI.motivation: While hybrid sequence models combining Transformers and state-space models are gaining interest, there's limited understanding of when and why they outperform constituent models. The paper aims to provide theoretical and empirical foundations for understanding hybrid model advantages.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of core synthetic tasks, proving limitations of non-hybrid models and constructing provably efficient hybrid models for selective copying and associative recall. Experimental validation with learned hybrids on broader settings.
AI.result:
1) Non-hybrid models require either large parameters or large working memory for fundamental tasks
2) Constructed hybrid models solve tasks with small size and working memory
3) Learned hybrids outperform non-hybrids with up to 6x more parameters
4) Hybrids show stronger length generalization and out-of-distribution robustness
AI.conclusion: Hybrid Transformer-state-space models overcome fundamental limitations of their constituent models, offering parameter efficiency and superior performance across synthetic tasks while demonstrating enhanced generalization capabilities beyond theoretical settings.

title: Time, Identity and Consciousness in Language Model Agents
comment: Accepted at AAAI 2026 Spring Symposium - Machine Consciousness: Integrating Theory, Technology, and Philosophy
summary: Machine consciousness evaluations mostly see behavior. For language model agents that behavior is language and tool use. That lets an agent say the right things about itself even when the constraints that should make those statements matter are not jointly present at decision time. We apply Stack Theory's temporal gap to scaffold trajectories. This separates ingredient-wise occurrence within an evaluation window from co-instantiation at a single objective step. We then instantiate Stack Theory's Arpeggio and Chord postulates on grounded identity statements. This yields two persistence scores that can be computed from instrumented scaffold traces. We connect these scores to five operational identity metrics and map common scaffolds into an identity morphospace that exposes predictable tradeoffs. The result is a conservative toolkit for identity evaluation. It separates talking like a stable self from being organized like one.

AI.tldr: Develops a framework to evaluate machine consciousness by distinguishing between an agent's ability to talk about having a stable identity and actually having the organizational structure of one, using persistence scores derived from scaffold trajectories.
AI.motivation: Current machine consciousness evaluations focus on behavior (language and tool use), allowing agents to say the right things about themselves even when the underlying constraints for genuine identity aren't present at decision time. This creates a gap between appearance and reality in identity evaluation.
AI.method: Applies Stack Theory's temporal gap to scaffold trajectories, separating ingredient occurrence within an evaluation window from co-instantiation at decision points. Instantiates Stack Theory's Arpeggio and Chord postulates on grounded identity statements to derive two persistence scores from instrumented scaffold traces.
AI.result: Creates a conservative toolkit for identity evaluation with two persistence scores, connects them to five operational identity metrics, maps common scaffolds into an identity morphospace revealing predictable tradeoffs, and separates talk about stable self from organizational reality.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a more rigorous framework for evaluating machine consciousness by distinguishing linguistic behavior from genuine organizational structure, offering measurable persistence scores and exposing tradeoffs in identity evaluation scaffolds.

title: A New Modeling to Feature Selection Based on the Fuzzy Rough Set Theory in Normal and Optimistic States on Hybrid Information Systems
comment: 18 pages, 14 figures, 9 tables. Published version available at International Journal of Engineering. This preprint is distributed under CC BY 4.0 license
summary: Considering the high volume, wide variety, and rapid speed of data generation, investigating feature selection methods for big data presents various applications and advantages. By removing irrelevant and redundant features, feature selection reduces data dimensions, thereby facilitating optimal decision-making within decision systems. One of the key tools for feature selection in hybrid information systems is fuzzy rough set theory. However, this theory faces two significant challenges: First, obtaining fuzzy equivalence relations through intersection operations in high-dimensional spaces can be both time-consuming and memory-intensive. Additionally, this method may produce noisy data, complicating the feature selection process. The purpose and innovation of this paper are to address these issues. We proposed a new feature selection model that calculates the combined distance between objects and subsequently used this information to derive the fuzzy equivalence relation. Rather than directly solving the feature selection problem, this approach reformulates it into an optimization problem that can be tackled using appropriate meta-heuristic algorithms. We have named this new approach FSbuHD. The FSbuHD model operates in two modes - normal and optimistic - based on the selection of one of the two introduced fuzzy equivalence relations. The model is then tested on standard datasets from the UCI repository and compared with other algorithms. The results of this research demonstrate that FSbuHD is one of the most efficient and effective methods for feature selection when compared to previous methods and algorithms.

AI.tldr: Proposed FSbuHD, a novel feature selection method using combined distance calculations and fuzzy equivalence relations in two modes (normal/optimistic), reformulated as an optimization problem for big data.
AI.motivation: Feature selection is crucial for big data (high volume, variety, speed) to reduce dimensions and facilitate decision-making. Current fuzzy rough set theory faces challenges: time-consuming memory-intensive intersection operations for fuzzy equivalence relations in high-dimensional spaces, and noisy data generation complicating feature selection.
AI.method: FSbuHD model calculates combined distance between objects and uses this to derive fuzzy equivalence relation. Instead of direct feature selection, reformulates as optimization problem solvable by meta-heuristic algorithms. Operates in two modes (normal/optimistic) based on two introduced fuzzy equivalence relations.
AI.result: Tested on standard UCI datasets and compared with other algorithms. Results show FSbuHD is one of the most efficient and effective methods for feature selection compared to previous methods.
AI.conclusion: FSbuHD successfully addresses the limitations of traditional fuzzy rough set theory by providing a more efficient and effective feature selection approach for big data applications through distance-based calculations and optimization reformulation.

title: EPOCH: An Agentic Protocol for Multi-Round System Optimization
summary: Autonomous agents are increasingly used to improve prompts, code, and machine learning systems through iterative execution and feedback. Yet existing approaches are usually designed as task-specific optimization loops rather than as a unified protocol for establishing baselines and managing tracked multi-round self-improvement. We introduce EPOCH, an engineering protocol for multi-round system optimization in heterogeneous environments. EPOCH organizes optimization into two phases: baseline construction and iterative self-improvement. It further structures each round through role-constrained stages that separate planning, implementation, and evaluation, and standardizes execution through canonical command interfaces and round-level tracking. This design enables coordinated optimization across prompts, model configurations, code, and rule-based components while preserving stability, reproducibility, traceability, and integrity of evaluation. Empirical studies in various tasks illustrate the practicality of EPOCH for production-oriented autonomous improvement workflows.

AI.tldr: EPOCH is a unified engineering protocol for multi-round optimization of autonomous AI systems, organizing workflows into baseline construction and iterative self-improvement phases with standardized tracking.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous agent approaches are task-specific optimization loops lacking unified protocols for establishing baselines and managing tracked multi-round self-improvement across heterogeneous environments.
AI.method: EPOCH organizes optimization into two phases: baseline construction and iterative self-improvement. Each round uses role-constrained stages (planning, implementation, evaluation) with canonical command interfaces and round-level tracking for standardization.
AI.result: The protocol enables coordinated optimization across prompts, model configurations, code, and rule-based components while preserving stability, reproducibility, traceability, and evaluation integrity across various tasks.
AI.conclusion: EPOCH provides a practical engineering protocol for production-oriented autonomous improvement workflows, addressing the need for standardized multi-round optimization in heterogeneous environments.

title: Cross-Domain Uncertainty Quantification for Selective Prediction: A Comprehensive Bound Ablation with Transfer-Informed Betting
summary: We present a comprehensive ablation of nine finite-sample bound families for selective prediction with risk control, combining concentration inequalities (Hoeffding, Empirical Bernstein, Clopper-Pearson, Wasserstein DRO, CVaR) with multiple-testing corrections (union bound, Learn Then Test fixed-sequence) and betting-based confidence sequences (WSR).
