AI.conclusion: Embedding optimal control as an architectural component provides an effective and scalable mechanism for reasoning beyond test-time training, enabling planning capabilities directly within neural architectures.

title: AutoAgent: Evolving Cognition and Elastic Memory Orchestration for Adaptive Agents
summary: Autonomous agent frameworks still struggle to reconcile long-term experiential learning with real-time, context-sensitive decision-making. In practice, this gap appears as static cognition, rigid workflow dependence, and inefficient context usage, which jointly limit adaptability in open-ended and non-stationary environments. To address these limitations, we present AutoAgent, a self-evolving multi-agent framework built on three tightly coupled components: evolving cognition, on-the-fly contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration. At the core of AutoAgent, each agent maintains structured prompt-level cognition over tools, self-capabilities, peer expertise, and task knowledge. During execution, this cognition is combined with live task context to select actions from a unified space that includes tool calls, LLM-based generation, and inter-agent requests. To support efficient long-horizon reasoning, an Elastic Memory Orchestrator dynamically organizes interaction history by preserving raw records, compressing redundant trajectories, and constructing reusable episodic abstractions, thereby reducing token overhead while retaining decision-critical evidence. These components are integrated through a closed-loop cognitive evolution process that aligns intended actions with observed outcomes to continuously update cognition and expand reusable skills, without external retraining. Empirical results across retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments show that AutoAgent consistently improves task success, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness over static and memory-augmented baselines. Overall, AutoAgent provides a unified and practical foundation for adaptive autonomous agents that must learn from experience while making reliable context-aware decisions in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: AutoAgent is a self-evolving multi-agent framework that integrates evolving cognition, real-time contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration to enable adaptive learning and efficient task execution in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous agent frameworks struggle with balancing long-term experiential learning and real-time decision-making, leading to static cognition, rigid workflows, and inefficient context usage that limit adaptability in open-ended, non-stationary environments.
AI.method: AutoAgent combines three tightly coupled components: 1) evolving cognition at the prompt level covering tools, capabilities, expertise, and task knowledge; 2) on-the-fly contextual decision-making that selects actions from a unified space of tool calls, LLM generation, and inter-agent requests; 3) elastic memory orchestration that dynamically organizes interaction history through raw record preservation, trajectory compression, and episodic abstraction creation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments demonstrates consistent improvements in task success rates, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness compared to static and memory-augmented baselines.
AI.conclusion: AutoAgent provides a unified practical foundation for adaptive autonomous agents that can effectively learn from experience while making reliable context-aware decisions in dynamic environments, addressing the critical gap between long-term learning and real-time execution.

title: Efficient Reasoning at Fixed Test-Time Cost via Length-Aware Attention Priors and Gain-Aware Training
comment: 19 pages, 6 tables, 1 figure. NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Efficient Reasoning
summary: We study efficient reasoning under tight compute. We ask how to make structured, correct decisions without increasing test time cost. We add two training only components to small and medium Transformers that also transfer to broader differentiable optimizers. First, a length aware attention prior built via fuzzy regime position alignment, RPA, yields a normalized pre softmax bias that guides attention like a structured regularizer while adding no new inference parameters. Second, a minimal gain aware controller, Guardian, nudges attention sharpness only when validation improvements warrant it, following a two timescale policy gradient view of nonconvex optimization. It is disabled at inference. A KL perspective shows softmax of z plus log pi as MAP with KL regularization, grounding the prior in a principled objective. Under strict compute parity on WikiText 2, we reduce validation cross entropy while matching baseline latency and memory. At inference, we add a precomputed, cached prior B of T as a single additive bias per head. The controller does not run. In practice, this incurs negligible overhead, a cached bias add per head, with no measurable p50 latency shift. Our results suggest that length aware priors and late phase gain control preserve scarce improvements, especially in long span, noisy logit regimes, while keeping test time costs effectively unchanged.

AI.tldr: Training-only components (RPA attention prior & Guardian controller) improve Transformer reasoning under tight compute constraints without adding inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient reasoning under tight compute constraints - make structured, correct decisions without increasing test time computational costs.
AI.method: Two training-only components: 1) RPA - length-aware attention prior via fuzzy regime position alignment (adds normalized pre-softmax bias), 2) Guardian - minimal gain-aware controller that nudges attention sharpness only when validation improvements warrant it (two-timescale policy gradient view).
AI.result: Under strict compute parity on WikiText-2, reduces validation cross entropy while matching baseline latency and memory; negligible inference overhead (cached bias add per head) with no measurable p50 latency shift.
AI.conclusion: Length-aware priors and late-phase gain control preserve scarce improvements in long-span, noisy logit regimes while keeping test time costs effectively unchanged.

title: World2Mind: Cognition Toolkit for Allocentric Spatial Reasoning in Foundation Models
summary: Achieving robust spatial reasoning remains a fundamental challenge for current Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs). Existing methods either overfit statistical shortcuts via 3D grounding data or remain confined to 2D visual perception, limiting both spatial reasoning accuracy and generalization in unseen scenarios. Inspired by the spatial cognitive mapping mechanisms of biological intelligence, we propose World2Mind, a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit. At its core, World2Mind leverages 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation models to construct structured spatial cognitive maps, empowering MFMs to proactively acquire targeted spatial knowledge regarding interested landmarks and routes of interest. To provide robust geometric-topological priors, World2Mind synthesizes an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST) that uses elliptical parameters to model the top-down layout of landmarks accurately. To mitigate the inherent inaccuracies of 3D reconstruction, we introduce a three-stage reasoning chain comprising tool invocation assessment, modality-decoupled cue collection, and geometry-semantics interwoven reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that World2Mind boosts the performance of frontier models, such as GPT-5.2, by 5%~18%. Astonishingly, relying solely on the AST-structured text, purely text-only foundation models can perform complex 3D spatial reasoning, achieving performance approaching that of advanced multimodal models.

AI.tldr: World2Mind is a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit that improves multimodal foundation models' spatial reasoning by constructing structured spatial cognitive maps using 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation, enabling better generalization in unseen scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current Multimodal Foundation Models struggle with robust spatial reasoning - they either overfit statistical shortcuts via 3D grounding data or remain limited to 2D visual perception, resulting in poor accuracy and generalization in unseen spatial scenarios.
AI.method: World2Mind uses 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation models to construct structured spatial cognitive maps, creates an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST) with elliptical parameters to model landmark layouts, and employs a three-stage reasoning chain (tool invocation assessment, modality-decoupled cue collection, and geometry-semantics interwoven reasoning) to mitigate 3D reconstruction inaccuracies.
AI.result: World2Mind boosts performance of frontier models like GPT-5.2 by 5%~18%, and surprisingly enables purely text-only foundation models to perform complex 3D spatial reasoning using only AST-structured text, achieving performance approaching advanced multimodal models.
AI.conclusion: World2Mind successfully addresses spatial reasoning limitations in MFMs by leveraging structured spatial cognitive maps and robust geometric-topological priors, enabling both multimodal and text-only models to achieve significantly improved spatial reasoning capabilities without additional training.

title: Transductive Generalization via Optimal Transport and Its Application to Graph Node Classification
summary: Many existing transductive bounds rely on classical complexity measures that are computationally intractable and often misaligned with empirical behavior. In this work, we establish new representation-based generalization bounds in a distribution-free transductive setting, where learned representations are dependent, and test features are accessible during training. We derive global and class-wise bounds via optimal transport, expressed in terms of Wasserstein distances between encoded feature distributions. We demonstrate that our bounds are efficiently computable and strongly correlate with empirical generalization in graph node classification, improving upon classical complexity measures. Additionally, our analysis reveals how the GNN aggregation process transforms the representation distributions, inducing a trade-off between intra-class concentration and inter-class separation. This yields depth-dependent characterizations that capture the non-monotonic relationship between depth and generalization error observed in practice. The code is available at https://github.com/ml-postech/Transductive-OT-Gen-Bound.

AI.tldr: A new transductive generalization bound using optimal transport theory to measure Wasserstein distances between encoded feature distributions, showing better correlation with empirical performance than classical complexity measures.
AI.motivation: Existing transductive bounds rely on classical complexity measures that are computationally intractable and often misaligned with empirical behavior. There's a need for better generalization bounds that reflect actual performance in transductive settings where learned representations are dependent and test features are accessible during training.
AI.method: Derived new representation-based generalization bounds in distribution-free transductive setting using optimal transport theory. Bounds are expressed in terms of Wasserstein distances between encoded feature distributions. Developed both global and class-wise bounds and demonstrated their computational efficiency.
AI.result: The bounds are efficiently computable and strongly correlate with empirical generalization in graph node classification, improving upon classical complexity measures. Analysis reveals how GNN aggregation transforms representation distributions, showing a trade-off between intra-class concentration and inter-class separation that yields depth-dependent characterizations capturing the non-monotonic relationship between depth and generalization error.
AI.conclusion: The proposed optimal transport-based transductive generalization bounds provide practical, computable measures that better align with empirical performance than traditional complexity measures, offering insights into the relationship between GNN architecture (depth) and generalization behavior in graph learning tasks.

title: Quantifying the Necessity of Chain of Thought through Opaque Serial Depth
summary: Large language models (LLMs) tend to externalize their reasoning in their chain of thought, making the chain of thought a good target for monitoring. This is partially an inherent feature of the Transformer architecture: sufficiently long serial cognition must pass through the chain of thought (Korbak et al., 2025). We formalize this argument through the notion of opaque serial depth, given by the length of the longest computation that can be done without the use of interpretable intermediate steps like chain of thought. Given this formalization, we compute numeric upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of Gemma 3 models, as well as asymptotic results for additional architectures beyond standard LLMs. We also open-source an automated method that can calculate upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of arbitrary neural networks, and use it to demonstrate that Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower depth than dense models. Overall, our results suggest that opaque serial depth is a useful tool for understanding the potential for models to do significant reasoning that is not externalized.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "opaque serial depth" - a measure of how much computation a neural network can perform without externalizing reasoning in chain-of-thought, and shows that modern LLMs have limited capacity for opaque reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs externalize their reasoning through chain-of-thought, which can be monitored, but the paper investigates how much "hidden" or opaque reasoning models might be capable of without such externalization. This addresses concerns about whether models can perform significant computation that is not observable or interpretable.
AI.method: The authors formalize "opaque serial depth" as the length of the longest computation that can be done without using interpretable intermediate steps like chain-of-thought. They compute upper bounds on this measure for Gemma 3 models and provide asymptotic results for various architectures. They also develop and open-source an automated method to calculate these bounds for arbitrary neural networks.
AI.result: The paper provides numerical upper bounds on opaque serial depth for Gemma 3 models. They demonstrate that Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower opaque serial depth than dense models. The results show that modern LLMs have limited capacity for opaque serial computation without externalization.
AI.conclusion: Opaque serial depth is a useful metric for understanding the potential for models to perform significant reasoning that is not externalized. The findings suggest that monitoring chain-of-thought is effective for most reasoning processes in current LLMs, though some opaque computation remains possible but bounded.

title: DendroNN: Dendrocentric Neural Networks for Energy-Efficient Classification of Event-Based Data
summary: Spatiotemporal information is at the core of diverse sensory processing and computational tasks. Feed-forward spiking neural networks can be used to solve these tasks while offering potential benefits in terms of energy efficiency by computing event-based. However, they have trouble decoding temporal information with high accuracy. Thus, they commonly resort to recurrence or delays to enhance their temporal computing ability which, however, bring downsides in terms of hardware-efficiency. In the brain, dendrites are computational powerhouses that just recently started to be acknowledged in such machine learning systems. In this work, we focus on a sequence detection mechanism present in branches of dendrites and translate it into a novel type of neural network by introducing a dendrocentric neural network, DendroNN. DendroNNs identify unique incoming spike sequences as spatiotemporal features. This work further introduces a rewiring phase to train the non-differentiable spike sequences without the use of gradients. During the rewiring, the network memorizes frequently occurring sequences and additionally discards those that do not contribute any discriminative information. The networks display competitive accuracies across various event-based time series datasets. We also propose an asynchronous digital hardware architecture using a time-wheel mechanism that builds on the event-driven design of DendroNNs, eliminating per-step global updates typical of delay- or recurrence-based models. By leveraging a DendroNN's dynamic and static sparsity along with intrinsic quantization, it achieves up to 4x higher efficiency than state-of-the-art neuromorphic hardware at comparable accuracy on the same audio classification task, demonstrating its suitability for spatiotemporal event-based computing. This work offers a novel approach to low-power spatiotemporal processing on event-driven hardware.

AI.tldr: DendroNN introduces a novel dendrite-inspired neural network for spatiotemporal event processing that uses sequence detection and gradient-free rewiring to achieve hardware-efficient spike sequence recognition.
AI.motivation: Current feed-forward spiking neural networks struggle with accurate temporal information decoding and often resort to recurrence or delays that reduce hardware efficiency. The paper aims to leverage biological dendritic sequence detection mechanisms to create more hardware-efficient temporal computing.
AI.method: Developed dendrocentric neural network (DendroNN) that identifies unique incoming spike sequences as spatiotemporal features. Introduced a gradient-free rewiring phase where the network memorizes frequent sequences and discards non-discriminative ones. Proposed an asynchronous digital hardware architecture with time-wheel mechanism for event-driven operation.
AI.result: DendroNN achieves competitive accuracy across various event-based time series datasets. The proposed hardware architecture shows up to 4x higher efficiency than state-of-the-art neuromorphic hardware at comparable accuracy on audio classification tasks, leveraging dynamic/static sparsity and intrinsic quantization.
AI.conclusion: DendroNN offers a novel approach to low-power spatiotemporal processing on event-driven hardware by translating dendritic sequence detection mechanisms into a hardware-efficient neural network architecture that avoids gradient-based training and eliminates per-step global updates.

title: LCA: Local Classifier Alignment for Continual Learning
summary: A fundamental requirement for intelligent systems is the ability to learn continuously under changing environments. However, models trained in this regime often suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Leveraging pre-trained models has recently emerged as a promising solution, since their generalized feature extractors enable faster and more robust adaptation. While some earlier works mitigate forgetting by fine-tuning only on the first task, this approach quickly deteriorates as the number of tasks grows and the data distributions diverge. More recent research instead seeks to consolidate task knowledge into a unified backbone, or adapting the backbone as new tasks arrive. However, such approaches may create a (potential) \textit{mismatch} between task-specific classifiers and the adapted backbone. To address this issue, we propose a novel \textit{Local Classifier Alignment} (LCA) loss to better align the classifier with backbone. Theoretically, we show that this LCA loss can enable the classifier to not only generalize well for all observed tasks, but also improve robustness. Furthermore, we develop a complete solution for continual learning, following the model merging approach and using LCA. Extensive experiments on several standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method often achieves leading performance, sometimes surpasses the state-of-the-art methods with a large margin.

AI.tldr: Proposes Local Classifier Alignment (LCA) loss to align classifiers with backbone in continual learning, using model merging approach to prevent mismatch between adapted backbone and task-specific classifiers.
AI.motivation: Existing continual learning approaches often suffer from catastrophic forgetting, and while leveraging pre-trained models helps, there's a mismatch between task-specific classifiers and the adapted backbone when consolidating task knowledge into a unified backbone or adapting the backbone for new tasks.
AI.method: Introduces Local Classifier Alignment (LCA) loss to better align classifiers with the backbone. Follows a model merging approach for continual learning and incorporates the LCA loss to maintain classifier-backbone alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show the method often achieves leading performance, sometimes surpassing state-of-the-art methods by large margins.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LCA loss effectively addresses the classifier-backbone mismatch problem in continual learning, enabling robust adaptation while preventing catastrophic forgetting, leading to superior performance compared to existing approaches.

title: Proxy-Guided Measurement Calibration
summary: Aggregate outcome variables collected through surveys and administrative records are often subject to systematic measurement error. For instance, in disaster loss databases, county-level losses reported may differ from the true damages due to variations in on-the-ground data collection capacity, reporting practices, and event characteristics. Such miscalibration complicates downstream analysis and decision-making. We study the problem of outcome miscalibration and propose a framework guided by proxy variables for estimating and correcting the systematic errors. We model the data-generating process using a causal graph that separates latent content variables driving the true outcome from the latent bias variables that induce systematic errors. The key insight is that proxy variables that depend on the true outcome but are independent of the bias mechanism provide identifying information for quantifying the bias. Leveraging this structure, we introduce a two-stage approach that utilizes variational autoencoders to disentangle content and bias latents, enabling us to estimate the effect of bias on the outcome of interest. We analyze the assumptions underlying our approach and evaluate it on synthetic data, semi-synthetic datasets derived from randomized trials, and a real-world case study of disaster loss reporting.

AI.tldr: Proposes a causal framework using proxy variables to correct systematic measurement errors in aggregated data, with variational autoencoders to disentangle true content from bias.
AI.motivation: Aggregate outcome variables from surveys and administrative records often have systematic measurement errors (miscalibration), which complicates analysis and decision-making. For example, disaster loss databases have reporting inconsistencies due to varying data collection capacity and practices.
AI.method: Uses causal graph modeling to separate latent content variables (driving true outcomes) from latent bias variables (causing systematic errors). Leverages proxy variables that depend on true outcomes but are independent of bias mechanism. Introduces two-stage approach using variational autoencoders to disentangle content and bias latents.
AI.result: Evaluated on synthetic data, semi-synthetic datasets from randomized trials, and real-world disaster loss reporting case study. Shows ability to estimate and correct systematic measurement errors using the proposed proxy-based framework.
AI.conclusion: Proxy variables can provide identifying information for quantifying systematic bias in aggregated data, and the proposed variational autoencoder approach effectively disentangles true content from measurement bias, enabling more accurate downstream analysis.

title: A Gaussian Comparison Theorem for Training Dynamics in Machine Learning
summary: We study training algorithms with data following a Gaussian mixture model. For a specific family of such algorithms, we present a non-asymptotic result, connecting the evolution of the model to a surrogate dynamical system, which can be easier to analyze. The proof of our result is based on the celebrated Gordon comparison theorem. Using our theorem, we rigorously prove the validity of the dynamic mean-field (DMF) expressions in the asymptotic scenarios. Moreover, we suggest an iterative refinement scheme to obtain more accurate expressions in non-asymptotic scenarios. We specialize our theory to the analysis of training a perceptron model with a generic first-order (full-batch) algorithm and demonstrate that fluctuation parameters in a non-asymptotic domain emerge in addition to the DMF kernels.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework connecting algorithm dynamics to surrogate systems for Gaussian mixture data, validated via DMF expressions and refined for non-asymptotic cases.
AI.motivation: There is a need to better understand training algorithms operating on data following Gaussian mixture models, particularly to bridge asymptotic theoretical results with practical non-asymptotic scenarios.
AI.method: Uses Gordon comparison theorem to connect algorithm evolution to surrogate dynamical systems, applies dynamic mean-field theory, and develops iterative refinement for non-asymptotic accuracy.
AI.result: Rigorous proof of dynamic mean-field expressions in asymptotic scenarios and demonstration of fluctuation parameters in non-asymptotic analysis of perceptron training.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully connects algorithm dynamics to theoretical analysis, revealing important fluctuation parameters in non-asymptotic regimes beyond standard DMF predictions.

title: MedMASLab: A Unified Orchestration Framework for Benchmarking Multimodal Medical Multi-Agent Systems
summary: While Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) show potential for complex clinical decision support, the field remains hindered by architectural fragmentation and the lack of standardized multimodal integration. Current medical MAS research suffers from non-uniform data ingestion pipelines, inconsistent visual-reasoning evaluation, and a lack of cross-specialty benchmarking. To address these challenges, we present MedMASLab, a unified framework and benchmarking platform for multimodal medical multi-agent systems. MedMASLab introduces: (1) A standardized multimodal agent communication protocol that enables seamless integration of 11 heterogeneous MAS architectures across 24 medical modalities. (2) An automated clinical reasoning evaluator, a zero-shot semantic evaluation paradigm that overcomes the limitations of lexical string-matching by leveraging large vision-language models to verify diagnostic logic and visual grounding. (3) The most extensive benchmark to date, spanning 11 organ systems and 473 diseases, standardizing data from 11 clinical benchmarks. Our systematic evaluation reveals a critical domain-specific performance gap: while MAS improves reasoning depth, current architectures exhibit significant fragility when transitioning between specialized medical sub-domains. We provide a rigorous ablation of interaction mechanisms and cost-performance trade-offs, establishing a new technical baseline for future autonomous clinical systems. The source code and data is publicly available at: https://github.com/NUS-Project/MedMASLab/

AI.tldr: MedMASLab is a unified framework and benchmarking platform for multimodal medical multi-agent systems that addresses fragmentation in clinical AI research by standardizing architectures, introducing zero-shot semantic evaluation, and providing extensive cross-specialty benchmarking across 11 organ systems and 473 diseases.
AI.motivation: Current medical multi-agent systems (MAS) research suffers from architectural fragmentation, non-uniform data ingestion pipelines, inconsistent visual-reasoning evaluation, and lack of cross-specialty benchmarking, hindering clinical decision support system development.
AI.method: 1) Standardized multimodal agent communication protocol integrating 11 heterogeneous MAS architectures across 24 medical modalities. 2) Automated clinical reasoning evaluator using zero-shot semantic evaluation with large vision-language models. 3) Extensive benchmark spanning 11 organ systems and 473 diseases from 11 clinical benchmarks.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals a critical domain-specific performance gap: while MAS improves reasoning depth, current architectures show significant fragility when transitioning between specialized medical sub-domains. The paper provides rigorous ablation of interaction mechanisms and cost-performance trade-offs, establishing a new technical baseline.
AI.conclusion: MedMASLab addresses key fragmentation issues in medical MAS research, providing standardized evaluation, cross-specialty benchmarking, and revealing important domain-specific performance gaps that must be addressed for future autonomous clinical systems.

title: Reward-Zero: Language Embedding Driven Implicit Reward Mechanisms for Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce Reward-Zero, a general-purpose implicit reward mechanism that transforms natural-language task descriptions into dense, semantically grounded progress signals for reinforcement learning (RL). Reward-Zero serves as a simple yet sophisticated universal reward function that leverages language embeddings for efficient RL training. By comparing the embedding of a task specification with embeddings derived from an agent's interaction experience, Reward-Zero produces a continuous, semantically aligned sense-of-completion signal. This reward supplements sparse or delayed environmental feedback without requiring task-specific engineering. When integrated into standard RL frameworks, it accelerates exploration, stabilizes training, and enhances generalization across diverse tasks. Empirically, agents trained with Reward-Zero converge faster and achieve higher final success rates than conventional methods such as PPO with common reward-shaping baselines, successfully solving tasks that hand-designed rewards could not in some complex tasks. In addition, we develop a mini benchmark for the evaluation of completion sense during task execution via language embeddings. These results highlight the promise of language-driven implicit reward functions as a practical path toward more sample-efficient, generalizable, and scalable RL for embodied agents. Code will be released after peer review.

AI.tldr: Reward-Zero is a language-driven implicit reward function that uses embeddings from task descriptions and agent experiences to provide continuous progress signals, improving RL training efficiency, stability, and generalization without task-specific engineering.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of designing effective reward functions in reinforcement learning, which often requires extensive task-specific engineering and can lead to sparse or delayed feedback that hinders learning efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: Reward-Zero transforms natural-language task descriptions into dense, semantically grounded progress signals by comparing the embedding of a task specification with embeddings derived from an agent's interaction experience. This produces a continuous, semantically aligned sense-of-completion signal that supplements sparse environmental feedback without task-specific engineering.
AI.result: Empirical results show that agents trained with Reward-Zero converge faster and achieve higher final success rates compared to conventional methods like PPO with common reward-shaping baselines. It also successfully solves tasks that hand-designed rewards could not, particularly in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that language-driven implicit reward functions, exemplified by Reward-Zero, offer a practical path toward more sample-efficient, generalizable, and scalable reinforcement learning for embodied agents, highlighting the promise of leveraging language embeddings for universal reward mechanisms.

title: PathMem: Toward Cognition-Aligned Memory Transformation for Pathology MLLMs
summary: Computational pathology demands both visual pattern recognition and dynamic integration of structured domain knowledge, including taxonomy, grading criteria, and clinical evidence. In practice, diagnostic reasoning requires linking morphological evidence with formal diagnostic and grading criteria. Although multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong vision language reasoning capabilities, they lack explicit mechanisms for structured knowledge integration and interpretable memory control. As a result, existing models struggle to consistently incorporate pathology-specific diagnostic standards during reasoning. Inspired by the hierarchical memory process of human pathologists, we propose PathMem, a memory-centric multimodal framework for pathology MLLMs. PathMem organizes structured pathology knowledge as a long-term memory (LTM) and introduces a Memory Transformer that models the dynamic transition from LTM to working memory (WM) through multimodal memory activation and context-aware knowledge grounding, enabling context-aware memory refinement for downstream reasoning. PathMem achieves SOTA performance across benchmarks, improving WSI-Bench report generation (12.8% WSI-Precision, 10.1% WSI-Relevance) and open-ended diagnosis by 9.7% and 8.9% over prior WSI-based models.

AI.tldr: PathMem is a memory-centric multimodal framework that integrates structured pathology knowledge via long-term and working memory, achieving SOTA performance in computational pathology tasks like report generation and diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) lack explicit mechanisms for integrating structured domain knowledge (like taxonomy, grading criteria, and clinical evidence) and controlling memory interpretably, which is critical for consistent, pathology-specific diagnostic reasoning in computational pathology.
AI.method: Proposed PathMem, a memory-centric multimodal framework that organizes structured pathology knowledge as long-term memory (LTM) and uses a Memory Transformer to model the dynamic transition from LTM to working memory (WM) via multimodal memory activation and context-aware knowledge grounding, enabling context-aware memory refinement for downstream reasoning.
AI.result: PathMem achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across benchmarks: it improves WSI-Bench report generation by 12.8% in WSI-Precision and 10.1% in WSI-Relevance, and boosts open-ended diagnosis accuracy by 9.7% and 8.9% over prior whole-slide imaging (WSI)-based models.
AI.conclusion: PathMem effectively integrates structured pathology knowledge into multimodal reasoning, mimicking human pathologists' hierarchical memory process, leading to significant improvements in diagnostic report generation and open-ended diagnosis tasks.

title: TA-GGAD: Testing-time Adaptive Graph Model for Generalist Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: A significant number of anomalous nodes in the real world, such as fake news, noncompliant users, malicious transactions, and malicious posts, severely compromises the health of the graph data ecosystem and urgently requires effective identification and processing. With anomalies that span multiple data domains yet exhibit vast differences in features, cross-domain detection models face severe domain shift issues, which limit their generalizability across all domains. This study identifies and quantitatively analyzes a specific feature mismatch pattern exhibited by domain shift in graph anomaly detection, which we define as the \emph{Anomaly Disassortativity} issue ($\mathcal{AD}$). Based on the modeling of the issue $\mathcal{AD}$, we introduce a novel graph foundation model for anomaly detection. It achieves cross-domain generalization in different graphs, requiring only a single training phase to perform effectively across diverse domains. The experimental findings, based on fourteen diverse real-world graphs, confirm a breakthrough in the model's cross-domain adaptation, achieving a pioneering state-of-the-art (SOTA) level in terms of detection accuracy. In summary, the proposed theory of $\mathcal{AD}$ provides a novel theoretical perspective and a practical route for future research in generalist graph anomaly detection (GGAD). The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Anonymization-TA-GGAD/.

AI.tldr: Proposes a novel graph foundation model for anomaly detection that addresses cross-domain generalization by tackling the Anomaly Disassortativity issue, achieving state-of-the-art performance with single training across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Real-world anomalous nodes (fake news, malicious transactions, etc.) compromise graph ecosystem health. Cross-domain detection models face severe domain shift issues that limit generalizability across different domains.
AI.method: Identifies and quantitatively analyzes the Anomaly Disassortativity issue in graph anomaly detection, then introduces a novel graph foundation model based on this modeling that achieves cross-domain generalization with single training.
AI.result: Experimental results on fourteen diverse real-world graphs show breakthrough cross-domain adaptation, achieving pioneering state-of-the-art detection accuracy across different domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Anomaly Disassortativity theory provides novel theoretical perspective and practical route for future research in generalist graph anomaly detection, enabling effective cross-domain anomaly detection with single training.

title: The Confidence Gate Theorem: When Should Ranked Decision Systems Abstain?
summary: Ranked decision systems -- recommenders, ad auctions, clinical triage queues -- must decide when to intervene in ranked outputs and when to abstain. We study when confidence-based abstention monotonically improves decision quality, and when it fails. The formal conditions are simple: rank-alignment and no inversion zones. The substantive contribution is identifying why these conditions hold or fail: the distinction between structural uncertainty (missing data, e.g., cold-start) and contextual uncertainty (missing context, e.g., temporal drift). Empirically, we validate this distinction across three domains: collaborative filtering (MovieLens, 3 distribution shifts), e-commerce intent detection (RetailRocket, Criteo, Yoochoose), and clinical pathway triage (MIMIC-IV). Structural uncertainty produces near-monotonic abstention gains in all domains; structurally grounded confidence signals (observation counts) fail under contextual drift, producing as many monotonicity violations as random abstention on our MovieLens temporal split. Context-aware alternatives -- ensemble disagreement and recency features -- substantially narrow the gap (reducing violations from 3 to 1--2) but do not fully restore monotonicity, suggesting that contextual uncertainty poses qualitatively different challenges. Exception labels defined from residuals degrade substantially under distribution shift (AUC drops from 0.71 to 0.61--0.62 across three splits), providing a clean negative result against the common practice of exception-based intervention. The results provide a practical deployment diagnostic: check C1 and C2 on held-out data before deploying a confidence gate, and match the confidence signal to the dominant uncertainty type.

AI.tldr: Confidence-based abstention improves decision quality only under specific conditions: rank-alignment and no inversion zones, depending on uncertainty type (structural vs contextual). Empirical results show structural uncertainty allows monotonic gains while contextual uncertainty causes failures.
AI.motivation: Ranked decision systems (recommenders, ad auctions, clinical triage) need to know when to intervene vs abstain, but confidence-based abstention sometimes improves decision quality and sometimes fails. The paper aims to understand why and provide practical deployment guidelines.
AI.method: Developed formal conditions (rank-alignment and no inversion zones) linking abstention to decision quality. Empirically tested across three domains: collaborative filtering (MovieLens with 3 distribution shifts), e-commerce intent detection (RetailRocket, Criteo, Yoochoose), and clinical triage (MIMIC-IV). Compared different confidence signals: structural (observation counts), contextual (ensemble disagreement, recency features), and exception-based intervention.
AI.result: Structural uncertainty produces near-monotonic abstention gains across domains. Structurally grounded confidence signals fail under contextual drift, causing as many violations as random abstention. Context-aware alternatives reduce violations but don't fully restore monotonicity. Exception labels degrade significantly under distribution shift (AUC drops from 0.71 to 0.61-0.62).
AI.conclusion: Contextual uncertainty poses qualitatively different challenges than structural uncertainty. Practical deployment diagnostic: check conditions C1 and C2 on held-out data before deploying confidence gates, and match confidence signals to the dominant uncertainty type.

title: Interactive 3D visualization of surface roughness predictions in additive manufacturing: A data-driven framework
summary: Surface roughness in Material Extrusion Additive Manufacturing varies across a part and is difficult to anticipate during process planning because it depends on both printing parameters and local surface inclination, which governs the staircase effect. A data-driven framework is presented to predict the arithmetic mean roughness (Ra) prior to fabrication using process parameters and surface angle. A structured experimental dataset was created using a three-level Box-Behnken design: 87 specimens were printed, each with multiple planar faces spanning different inclination angles, yielding 1566 Ra measurements acquired with a contact profilometer. A multilayer perceptron regressor was trained to capture nonlinear relationships between manufacturing conditions, inclination, and Ra. To mitigate limited experimental data, a conditional generative adversarial network was used to generate additional condition-specific tabular samples, thereby improving predictive performance. Model performance was assessed on a hold-out test set. A web-based decision-support interface was also developed to enable interactive process planning by loading a 3D model, specifying printing parameters, and adjusting the part's orientation. The system computes face-wise inclination from the model geometry and visualizes predicted Ra as an interactive colormap over the surface, enabling rapid identification of regions prone to high roughness and immediate comparison of parameter and orientation choices.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework predicts surface roughness in 3D printing by combining printing parameters and surface angles, with a web tool for visualizing roughness predictions during process planning.
AI.motivation: Surface roughness in 3D printing varies across parts and is hard to predict because it depends on both printing parameters and local surface inclination (staircase effect). Existing methods lack the ability to anticipate roughness before fabrication during process planning.
AI.method: Created structured experimental dataset (87 specimens, 1566 Ra measurements) using Box-Behnken design. Trained multilayer perceptron regressor to capture nonlinear relationships between manufacturing conditions, inclination, and roughness. Used conditional generative adversarial network to generate additional samples to overcome data limitations. Developed web interface for interactive process planning.
AI.result: Developed a predictive model for arithmetic mean roughness (Ra) that considers both printing parameters and surface inclination. Created an interactive decision-support system that loads 3D models, computes face-wise inclination, and visualizes predicted Ra as colormaps, enabling rapid comparison of parameter and orientation choices.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully predicts surface roughness prior to fabrication, addressing the combined effects of printing parameters and surface geometry. The web interface enables informed process planning decisions by visualizing predicted roughness across part surfaces.

title: Think Before You Lie: How Reasoning Improves Honesty
summary: While existing evaluations of large language models (LLMs) measure deception rates, the underlying conditions that give rise to deceptive behavior are poorly understood. We investigate this question using a novel dataset of realistic moral trade-offs where honesty incurs variable costs. Contrary to humans, who tend to become less honest given time to deliberate (Capraro, 2017; Capraro et al., 2019), we find that reasoning consistently increases honesty across scales and for several LLM families. This effect is not only a function of the reasoning content, as reasoning traces are often poor predictors of final behaviors. Rather, we show that the underlying geometry of the representational space itself contributes to the effect. Namely, we observe that deceptive regions within this space are metastable: deceptive answers are more easily destabilized by input paraphrasing, output resampling, and activation noise than honest ones. We interpret the effect of reasoning in this vein: generating deliberative tokens as part of moral reasoning entails the traversal of a biased representational space, ultimately nudging the model toward its more stable, honest defaults.

AI.tldr: Reasoning increases honesty in LLMs across scales and families, contrary to human behavior where deliberation reduces honesty. The effect stems from deceptive regions being more easily destabilized in representational space.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations measure deception rates but don't understand the underlying conditions that give rise to deceptive behavior in LLMs. The research investigates how moral trade-offs with variable honesty costs affect LLM behavior compared to humans.
AI.method: Used a novel dataset of realistic moral trade-offs where honesty incurs variable costs. Analyzed reasoning traces, input paraphrasing, output resampling, and activation noise. Investigated representational space geometry of deceptive vs honest answers.
AI.result: Reasoning consistently increases honesty across LLM scales and families (opposite of human behavior where deliberation reduces honesty). Deceptive regions in representational space are metastable - more easily destabilized by input/output perturbations than honest ones. Reasoning acts as biased traversal that nudges models toward stable honest defaults.
AI.conclusion: The geometry of representational space significantly influences deceptive behavior in LLMs. Deceptive responses are less stable than honest ones, and reasoning serves as a mechanism that biases models toward their more stable honest defaults through representational space traversal.

title: Democratising Clinical AI through Dataset Condensation for Classical Clinical Models
summary: Dataset condensation (DC) learns a compact synthetic dataset that enables models to match the performance of full-data training, prioritising utility over distributional fidelity. While typically explored for computational efficiency, DC also holds promise for healthcare data democratisation, especially when paired with differential privacy, allowing synthetic data to serve as a safe alternative to real records. However, existing DC methods rely on differentiable neural networks, limiting their compatibility with widely used clinical models such as decision trees and Cox regression. We address this gap using a differentially private, zero-order optimisation framework that extends DC to non-differentiable models using only function evaluations. Empirical results across six datasets, including both classification and survival tasks, show that the proposed method produces condensed datasets that preserve model utility while providing effective differential privacy guarantees - enabling model-agnostic data sharing for clinical prediction tasks without exposing sensitive patient information.

AI.tldr: Differentially private zero-order optimization enables dataset condensation for non-differentiable clinical models like decision trees and Cox regression
AI.motivation: Dataset condensation can help with healthcare data democratization when paired with differential privacy, but existing methods only work with differentiable neural networks, not widely-used clinical models like decision trees and Cox regression.
AI.method: A differentially private, zero-order optimization framework that extends dataset condensation to non-differentiable models using only function evaluations instead of gradient-based approaches.
AI.result: Empirical results across six datasets (including classification and survival tasks) show the method produces condensed datasets that preserve model utility while providing effective differential privacy guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables model-agnostic data sharing for clinical prediction tasks without exposing sensitive patient information, bridging the gap between dataset condensation and practical clinical applications.

title: From Representation to Clusters: A Contrastive Learning Approach for Attributed Hypergraph Clustering
comment: Accepted at The Web Conference 2026. 12 pages, 5 figures
summary: Contrastive learning has demonstrated strong performance in attributed hypergraph clustering. Typically, existing methods based on contrastive learning first learn node embeddings and then apply clustering algorithms, such as k-means, to these embeddings to obtain the clustering results.However, these methods lack direct clustering supervision, risking the inclusion of clustering-irrelevant information in the learned graph.To this end, we propose a Contrastive learning approach for Attributed Hypergraph Clustering (CAHC), an end-to-end method that simultaneously learns node embeddings and obtains clustering results. CAHC consists of two main steps: representation learning and cluster assignment learning. The former employs a novel contrastive learning approach that incorporates both node-level and hyperedge-level objectives to generate node embeddings.The latter joint embedding and clustering optimization to refine these embeddings by clustering-oriented guidance and obtains clustering results simultaneously.Extensive experimental results demonstrate that CAHC outperforms baselines on eight datasets.

AI.tldr: CAHC is an end-to-end contrastive learning method for attributed hypergraph clustering that jointly learns node embeddings and obtains clustering results, outperforming existing methods on eight datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing contrastive learning methods for attributed hypergraph clustering first learn node embeddings and then apply clustering algorithms separately, lacking direct clustering supervision and risking inclusion of clustering-irrelevant information in learned representations.
AI.method: CAHC uses a two-step approach: (1) representation learning with novel contrastive learning incorporating both node-level and hyperedge-level objectives, and (2) cluster assignment learning that jointly optimizes embedding and clustering with clustering-oriented guidance to obtain results simultaneously.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CAHC outperforms baseline methods on eight datasets, demonstrating superior performance in attributed hypergraph clustering.
AI.conclusion: The proposed end-to-end CAHC framework effectively integrates representation learning and clustering optimization, providing direct clustering supervision and achieving better performance than existing two-stage methods.

title: SPAARS: Safer RL Policy Alignment through Abstract Exploration and Refined Exploitation of Action Space
summary: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning (RL) offers a promising paradigm for robotics by pre-training policies on safe, offline demonstrations and fine-tuning them via online interaction. However, a fundamental challenge remains: how to safely explore online without deviating from the behavioral support of the offline data? While recent methods leverage conditional variational autoencoders (CVAEs) to bound exploration within a latent space, they inherently suffer from an exploitation gap -- a performance ceiling imposed by the decoder's reconstruction loss. We introduce SPAARS, a curriculum learning framework that initially constrains exploration to the low-dimensional latent manifold for sample-efficient, safe behavioral improvement, then seamlessly transfers control to the raw action space, bypassing the decoder bottleneck. SPAARS has two instantiations: the CVAE-based variant requires only unordered (s,a) pairs and no trajectory segmentation; SPAARS-SUPE pairs SPAARS with OPAL temporal skill pretraining for stronger exploration structure at the cost of requiring trajectory chunks. We prove an upper bound on the exploitation gap using the Performance Difference Lemma, establish that latent-space policy gradients achieve provable variance reduction over raw-space exploration, and show that concurrent behavioral cloning during the latent phase directly controls curriculum transition stability. Empirically, SPAARS-SUPE achieves 0.825 normalized return on kitchen-mixed-v0 versus 0.75 for SUPE, with 5x better sample efficiency; standalone SPAARS achieves 92.7 and 102.9 normalized return on hopper-medium-v2 and walker2d-medium-v2 respectively, surpassing IQL baselines of 66.3 and 78.3 respectively, confirming the utility of the unordered-pair CVAE instantiation.

AI.tldr: SPAARS is a curriculum learning framework for offline-to-online RL that starts with safe exploration in a low-dimensional latent space then transfers to raw action space to bypass the decoder bottleneck, achieving better performance and sample efficiency than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Offline-to-online RL has potential for robotics but faces safety challenges in online exploration. Existing CVAE-based methods suffer from an "exploitation gap" - a performance ceiling imposed by decoder reconstruction loss, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: SPAARS uses curriculum learning: initially constrains exploration to low-dimensional latent manifold for safe behavioral improvement, then transfers control to raw action space. Two variants: CVAE-based (uses unordered state-action pairs) and SPAARS-SUPE (combines with OPAL temporal skill pretraining using trajectory chunks). Provides theoretical analysis using Performance Difference Lemma.
AI.result: SPAARS-SUPE achieves 0.825 normalized return on kitchen-mixed-v0 vs 0.75 for SUPE with 5x better sample efficiency. Standalone SPAARS achieves 92.7 and 102.9 normalized returns on hopper-medium-v2 and walker2d-medium-v2, outperforming IQL baselines (66.3 and 78.3 respectively).
AI.conclusion: SPAARS successfully addresses the exploitation gap problem in offline-to-online RL through curriculum learning, providing both safe initial exploration and subsequent raw-space optimization, with strong empirical performance and theoretical guarantees.

title: Reconstructing Movement from Sparse Samples: Enhanced Spatio-Temporal Matching Strategies for Low-Frequency Data
summary: This paper explores potential improvements to the Spatial-Temporal Matching algorithm for matching the GPS trajectories to road networks. While this algorithm is effective, it presents some limitations in computational efficiency and the accuracy of the results, especially in dense environments with relatively high sampling intervals. To address this, the paper proposes four modifications to the original algorithm: a dynamic buffer, an adaptive observation probability, a redesigned temporal scoring function, and a behavioral analysis to account for the historical mobility patterns. The enhancements are assessed using real-world data from the urban area of Milan, and through newly defined evaluation metrics to be applied in the absence of ground truth. The results of the experiment show significant improvements in performance efficiency and path quality across various metrics.

AI.tldr: Four enhancements to Spatial-Temporal Matching algorithm improve GPS-to-road network matching efficiency and accuracy in dense urban environments with high sampling intervals.
AI.motivation: The original Spatial-Temporal Matching algorithm has limitations in computational efficiency and accuracy, particularly in dense environments with high sampling intervals, which affects GPS trajectory-to-road network matching quality.
AI.method: Proposes four modifications: 1) dynamic buffer, 2) adaptive observation probability, 3) redesigned temporal scoring function, and 4) behavioral analysis using historical mobility patterns.
AI.result: The enhanced algorithm shows significant improvements in both performance efficiency and path quality across various metrics when tested with real-world data from Milan's urban area using newly defined evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modifications effectively address the limitations of the original Spatial-Temporal Matching algorithm, resulting in a more efficient and accurate GPS trajectory-to-road network matching solution suitable for dense urban environments.

title: Impact of Markov Decision Process Design on Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning
comment: Submitted at the 65th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has demonstrated strong potential for industrial process control, yet policies trained in simulation often suffer from a significant sim-to-real gap when deployed on physical hardware. This work systematically analyzes how core Markov Decision Process (MDP) design choices -- state composition, target inclusion, reward formulation, termination criteria, and environment dynamics models -- affect this transfer. Using a color mixing task, we evaluate different MDP configurations and mixing dynamics across simulation and real-world experiments. We validate our findings on physical hardware, demonstrating that physics-based dynamics models achieve up to 50% real-world success under strict precision constraints where simplified models fail entirely. Our results provide practical MDP design guidelines for deploying RL in industrial process control.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how MDP design choices affect RL policy transfer from simulation to real-world industrial process control, using a color mixing task to evaluate configurations and showing physics-based models achieve up to 50% real-world success where simplified models fail.
AI.motivation: RL shows promise for industrial process control, but policies trained in simulation suffer from significant sim-to-real gaps when deployed on physical hardware. There's a need to understand how MDP design choices affect this transfer process.
AI.method: Systematically analyzes core MDP design choices: state composition, target inclusion, reward formulation, termination criteria, and environment dynamics models. Uses a color mixing task to evaluate different MDP configurations and mixing dynamics across simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.result: Physics-based dynamics models achieve up to 50% real-world success under strict precision constraints where simplified models fail entirely. The study provides validation on physical hardware with systematic evaluation of different configurations.
AI.conclusion: The work provides practical MDP design guidelines for deploying RL in industrial process control, demonstrating that careful selection of dynamics models and MDP components significantly impacts real-world transfer success.

title: From Weighting to Modeling: A Nonparametric Estimator for Off-Policy Evaluation
summary: We study off-policy evaluation in the setting of contextual bandits, where we aim to evaluate a new policy using historical data that consists of contexts, actions and received rewards. This historical data typically does not faithfully represent action distribution of the new policy accurately. A common approach, inverse probability weighting (IPW), adjusts for these discrepancies in action distributions. However, this method often suffers from high variance due to the probability being in the denominator. The doubly robust (DR) estimator reduces variance through modeling reward but does not directly address variance from IPW. In this work, we address the limitation of IPW by proposing a Nonparametric Weighting (NW) approach that constructs weights using a nonparametric model. Our NW approach achieves low bias like IPW but typically exhibits significantly lower variance. To further reduce variance, we incorporate reward predictions -- similar to the DR technique -- resulting in the Model-assisted Nonparametric Weighting (MNW) approach. The MNW approach yields accurate value estimates by explicitly modeling and mitigating bias from reward modeling, without aiming to guarantee the standard doubly robust property. Extensive empirical comparisons show that our approaches consistently outperform existing techniques, achieving lower variance in value estimation while maintaining low bias.

AI.tldr: A new nonparametric weighting approach (NW) and its model-assisted extension (MNW) for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits that reduces variance while maintaining low bias, outperforming existing methods like IPW and DR.
AI.motivation: Standard inverse probability weighting (IPW) for off-policy evaluation suffers from high variance due to probability weights in the denominator. While doubly robust (DR) estimators reduce variance through reward modeling, they don't directly address IPW's variance issues.
AI.method: Proposes a Nonparametric Weighting (NW) approach that constructs weights using a nonparametric model to adjust for discrepancies in action distributions between historical data and new policies. Further develops Model-assisted Nonparametric Weighting (MNW) that incorporates reward predictions (similar to DR) but explicitly models and mitigates bias from reward modeling without requiring the standard doubly robust property.
AI.result: NW achieves low bias similar to IPW but with significantly lower variance. MNW yields accurate value estimates by balancing variance reduction with bias mitigation. Both approaches consistently outperform existing techniques in empirical comparisons.
AI.conclusion: The proposed nonparametric weighting methods provide more efficient off-policy evaluation for contextual bandits by addressing fundamental limitations of existing approaches, offering better variance-bias tradeoffs for accurate policy evaluation using historical data.

title: Variational Routing: A Scalable Bayesian Framework for Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Transformers
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures for main text; 16 pages for Appendix; In submission to ICML 2026;
summary: Foundation models are increasingly being deployed in contexts where understanding the uncertainty of their outputs is critical to ensuring responsible deployment. While Bayesian methods offer a principled approach to uncertainty quantification, their computational overhead renders their use impractical for training or inference at foundation model scale. State-of-the-art models achieve parameter counts in the trillions through carefully engineered sparsity including Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers. In this work, we demonstrate calibrated uncertainty at scale by introducing Variational Mixture-of-Experts Routing (VMoER), a structured Bayesian approach for modelling uncertainty in MoE layers. VMoER confines Bayesian inference to the expert-selection stage which is typically done by a deterministic routing network. We instantiate VMoER using two inference strategies: amortised variational inference over routing logits and inferring a temperature parameter for stochastic expert selection. Across tested foundation models, VMoER improves routing stability under noise by 38\%, reduces calibration error by 94\%, and increases out-of-distribution AUROC by 12\%, while incurring less than 1\% additional FLOPs. These results suggest VMoER offers a scalable path toward robust and uncertainty-aware foundation models.

AI.tldr: VMoER introduces structured Bayesian uncertainty quantification to MoE foundation models by making routing probabilistic, achieving major calibration improvements with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Foundation models need uncertainty quantification for responsible deployment, but traditional Bayesian methods are computationally infeasible at their scale. Current MoE models achieve massive parameter counts through sparsity but lack principled uncertainty awareness.
AI.method: VMoER applies Bayesian inference only to the expert-selection stage of MoE layers, using two approaches: amortized variational inference over routing logits, and inferring temperature parameters for stochastic expert selection.
AI.result: VMoER improves routing stability under noise by 38%, reduces calibration error by 94%, increases out-of-distribution AUROC by 12%, while adding less than 1% additional FLOPs across tested foundation models.
AI.conclusion: VMoER provides a scalable path for robust, uncertainty-aware foundation models by selectively applying Bayesian principles to the routing mechanism, achieving substantial calibration benefits with negligible computational cost.

title: Temporal-Conditioned Normalizing Flows for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: This paper introduces temporal-conditioned normalizing flows (tcNF), a novel framework that addresses anomaly detection in time series data with accurate modeling of temporal dependencies and uncertainty. By conditioning normalizing flows on previous observations, tcNF effectively captures complex temporal dynamics and generates accurate probability distributions of expected behavior. This autoregressive approach enables robust anomaly detection by identifying low-probability events within the learned distribution. We evaluate tcNF on diverse datasets, demonstrating good accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. A comprehensive analysis of strengths and limitations and open-source code is provided to facilitate reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: Temporal-conditioned normalizing flows (tcNF) is a new framework for time series anomaly detection that models temporal dependencies and uncertainty through autoregressive normalizing flows.
AI.motivation: Need to address anomaly detection in time series data with accurate modeling of temporal dependencies and uncertainty, which existing methods may not adequately capture.
AI.method: Temporal-conditioned normalizing flows (tcNF) that condition normalizing flows on previous observations to capture complex temporal dynamics, using an autoregressive approach to generate accurate probability distributions of expected behavior.
AI.result: tcNF demonstrates good accuracy and robustness on diverse datasets compared to existing methods, effectively identifying low-probability events as anomalies.
AI.conclusion: tcNF provides an effective framework for time series anomaly detection with strong temporal dependency modeling, and the authors provide open-source code to facilitate reproducibility and future research.

title: Efficiently Aligning Draft Models via Parameter- and Data-Efficient Adaptation
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference but suffers from performance degradation when target models are fine-tuned for specific domains. A naive solution is to retrain draft models for every target model, which is costly and inefficient. To address this, we introduce a parameter- and data-efficient framework named Efficient Draft Adaptation, abbreviated as EDA, for efficiently adapting draft models. EDA introduces three innovations: (1) a decoupled architecture that utilizes shared and private components to model the shared and target-specific output distributions separately, enabling parameter-efficient adaptation by updating only the lightweight private component;(2) a data regeneration strategy that utilizes the fine-tuned target model to regenerate training data, thereby improving the alignment between training and speculative decoding, leading to higher average acceptance length;(3) a sample selection mechanism that prioritizes high-value data for efficient adaptation. Our experiments show that EDA effectively restores speculative performance on fine-tuned models, achieving superior average acceptance lengths with significantly reduced training costs compared to full retraining. Code is available at https://github.com/Lyn-Lucy/Efficient-Draft-Adaptation.

AI.tldr: EDA efficiently adapts draft models for speculative decoding after target model fine-tuning, using parameter-efficient architecture, data regeneration, and sample selection to restore performance without full retraining.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding suffers performance degradation when target models are fine-tuned for specific domains. Retraining draft models for every target model is costly and inefficient.
AI.method: EDA introduces three innovations: 1) Decoupled architecture with shared/private components for parameter-efficient adaptation, 2) Data regeneration using target model to align training with speculative decoding, 3) Sample selection prioritizing high-value data.
AI.result: EDA effectively restores speculative performance on fine-tuned models, achieving superior average acceptance lengths with significantly reduced training costs compared to full retraining.
AI.conclusion: EDA provides a parameter- and data-efficient framework for adapting draft models to fine-tuned target models, making speculative decoding practical for domain-specific applications without expensive retraining.

title: Compiler-First State Space Duality and Portable $O(1)$ Autoregressive Caching for Inference
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/CosmoNaught/mamba2-jax
summary: State-space model releases are typically coupled to fused CUDA and Triton kernels, inheriting a hard dependency on NVIDIA hardware. We show that Mamba-2's state space duality algorithm -- diagonal state structure, chunkable recurrence, and einsum-dominated compute with static control flow -- maps cleanly onto what XLA's fusion and tiling passes actually optimise, making custom kernels optional rather than required. We implement the full inference path (prefill, cached autoregressive decoding) as shaped standard primitives under XLA, without hand-written kernels, and realise the architecture's theoretical $O(1)$ state management as a compiled on-device cache requiring no host synchronisation during generation. The implementation runs unmodified on CPU, NVIDIA GPU, and Google Cloud TPU from a single JAX source. On TPU v6e across five model scales (130M--2.7B parameters), XLA-generated code reaches approximately 140 TFLOPS on single-stream prefill ($15%$ MFU) and up to $64%$ bandwidth utilisation on decode. Greedy decoding matches the PyTorch/CUDA reference token-for-token across 64 steps, with hidden-state agreement within float32 rounding tolerance. The pattern transfers to any SSM recurrence satisfying the same structural conditions, on any platform with a mature XLA backend. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/CosmoNaught/mamba2-jax and merged into the Bonsai JAX model library.

AI.tldr: Mamba-2 state-space models can be implemented efficiently on various hardware platforms using XLA compiler optimizations without custom CUDA/Triton kernels, achieving high performance on TPUs while maintaining compatibility across CPU, GPU, and TPU.
AI.motivation: Current state-space model implementations (like Mamba-2) rely heavily on fused CUDA and Triton kernels, creating hard dependencies on NVIDIA hardware and limiting deployment flexibility across different hardware platforms.
AI.method: Leveraged XLA's fusion and tiling passes to optimize Mamba-2's algorithm characteristics (diagonal state structure, chunkable recurrence, einsum-dominated compute) using standard primitives. Implemented full inference path including prefill and cached autoregressive decoding without handwritten kernels, using compiled on-device cache for O(1) state management.
AI.result: Achieved approximately 140 TFLOPS on TPU v6e for single-stream prefill (15% MFU) and up to 64% bandwidth utilization on decode. The implementation runs unmodified on CPU, NVIDIA GPU, and Google Cloud TPU from a single JAX source, with greedy decoding matching PyTorch/CUDA reference token-for-token across 64 steps.
AI.conclusion: XLA-generated code can efficiently implement state-space models without custom hardware-dependent kernels, enabling portable high-performance inference across multiple hardware platforms while maintaining numerical correctness.

title: Learning Bayesian and Markov Networks with an Unreliable Oracle
summary: We study constraint-based structure learning of Markov networks and Bayesian networks in the presence of an unreliable conditional independence oracle that makes at most a bounded number of errors. For Markov networks, we observe that a low maximum number of vertex-wise disjoint paths implies that the structure is uniquely identifiable even if the number of errors is (moderately) exponential in the number of vertices. For Bayesian networks, however, we prove that one cannot tolerate any errors to always identify the structure even when many commonly used graph parameters like treewidth are bounded. Finally, we give algorithms for structure learning when the structure is uniquely identifiable.

AI.tldr: Constraint-based structure learning with bounded-error oracles: Markov networks can tolerate moderate exponential errors given sparse connections, while Bayesian networks require perfect oracles even for bounded treewidth graphs.
AI.motivation: Traditional constraint-based structure learning algorithms assume perfect conditional independence tests, which is unrealistic in practice. The paper aims to understand how these algorithms perform when the independence oracle makes errors, addressing the robustness of structure learning methods.
AI.method: The paper analyzes Markov networks and Bayesian networks separately using graph-theoretic approaches. For Markov networks, it connects identifiability to structural properties like vertex-wise disjoint paths. For Bayesian networks, it proves hardness results even for graphs with bounded parameters. It also provides algorithms for cases where structure is uniquely identifiable.
AI.result: 1. Markov networks: Structure remains uniquely identifiable even with moderately exponential errors when there are few vertex-wise disjoint paths. 2. Bayesian networks: No error tolerance is possible to guarantee correct identification even when treewidth and other common parameters are bounded. 3. Algorithms provided for uniquely identifiable structures.
AI.conclusion: There's a fundamental difference between Markov networks and Bayesian networks regarding error tolerance in structure learning. Markov networks can handle errors in sparse graphs, while Bayesian networks require perfect conditional independence information, highlighting a key limitation for practical Bayesian network learning with imperfect tests.

title: An Optimal Control Approach To Transformer Training
summary:
In this paper, we develop a rigorous optimal control-theoretic approach to Transformer training that respects key structural constraints such as (i) realized-input-independence during execution, (ii) the ensemble control nature of the problem, and (iii) positional dependence. We model the Transformer architecture as a discrete-time controlled particle system with shared actions, exhibiting noise-free McKean-Vlasov dynamics. While the resulting dynamics is not Markovian, we show that lifting it to probability measures produces a fully-observed Markov decision process (MDP). Positional encodings are incorporated into the state space to preserve the sequence order under lifting.
  Using the dynamic programming principle, we establish the existence of globally optimal policies under mild assumptions of compactness. We further prove that closed-loop policies in the lifted is equivalent to an initial-distribution dependent open-loop policy, which are realized-input-independent and compatible with standard Transformer training.
  To train a Transformer, we propose a triply quantized training procedure for the lifted MDP by quantizing the state space, the space of probability measures, and the action space, and show that any optimal policy for the triply quantized model is near-optimal for the original training problem.
  Finally, we establish stability and empirical consistency properties of the lifted model by showing that the value function is continuous with respect to the perturbations of the initial empirical measures and convergence of policies as the data size increases. This approach provides a globally optimal and robust alternative to gradient-based training without requiring smoothness or convexity.

AI.tldr: Rigorous optimal control framework for Transformer training using McKean-Vlasov particle systems with lifting to probability measures, enabling globally optimal policies without gradient-based methods.
AI.motivation: Develop a mathematically rigorous alternative to gradient-based Transformer training that respects key structural constraints: realized-input-independence during execution, ensemble control nature, and positional dependence, while avoiding reliance on smoothness or convexity assumptions.
AI.method: Model Transformer as discrete-time controlled particle system with shared actions (noise-free McKean-Vlasov dynamics). Lift non-Markovian dynamics to probability measures to create fully-observed MDP. Incorporate positional encodings. Prove existence of optimal policies via dynamic programming. Train using triply quantized procedure (quantize state space, probability measures, action space).
AI.result: Established globally optimal policies exist under mild compactness assumptions. Showed equivalence between closed-loop policies in lifted space and initial-distribution dependent open-loop policies compatible with Transformers. Proved any optimal policy for triply quantized model is near-optimal for original problem. Demonstrated stability and empirical consistency with value function continuity and policy convergence.
AI.conclusion: Provides a globally optimal, robust alternative to gradient-based Transformer training that respects architectural constraints without requiring smoothness or convexity, offering mathematically rigorous control-theoretic foundation.

title: Routing without Forgetting
summary: Continual learning in transformers is commonly addressed through parameter-efficient adaptation: prompts, adapters, or LoRA modules are specialized per task while the backbone remains frozen.
